title: Chapter I: The Poison Beneath the Crown
synopsis: A royal feast turns into chaos when King Baelenys is poisoned and the Red Keep falls into lockdown as suspicion spreads.
content: The reign of King Baelenys, later remembered by many as the Cruel, entered its darkest chapter not on a battlefield, but within the walls of his own castle.
It began on the sixteenth day of the second moon, the very day dragon eggs were set to hatch.

The court had gathered in celebration, unaware that before the dragons of House Targaryen would draw their first breath, death itself would reach for the king. During the feast, King Baelenys was poisoned. Chaos swept through the castle as the monarch collapsed, and before the source of the poison could be uncovered, the Red Keep was placed under lockdown. No one was permitted to leave. Every servant, guard, and noble suddenly became a suspect.
Yet fate cared little for the fears of men.
As the castle descended into suspicion, the dragon eggs began to crack.

Fate smiled strangely upon Prince Visenor, whose single egg gave birth to three hatchlings. While many at court assumed all three dragons would belong to the heir, Prince Jacaelon, known to most simply as Jace, openly challenged the idea. Though envy lingered beneath his words, his argument was difficult to dismiss. Three dragons, he argued, could never truly bond with a single rider, nor should so much of House Targaryen's future rest in the hands of one prince. Better that the hatchlings strengthen the blood of the family as a whole than become ornaments to one man's prestige.
The disagreement planted yet another seed of division among the king's children.
That same night, suspicion spread through the Red Keep.

Believing Lady Rhaella, Visenor's wife, to be hiding something, Jace paid her an unexpected visit. His questions were calm, but carried the weight of veiled threats. Before dawn, however, it became clear that someone else had also taken an interest in Jace. A spy was discovered lurking near his chambers, captured by Ser Alester Dayne before he could escape. Though subjected to brutal interrogation, the prisoner revealed nothing.

King Baelenys survived, but only barely. A lesser man would have perished, but by the very next morning, pale and weakened, one eyelid hanging lower than the other, he returned to the Iron Throne with death still lingering in his eyes. The court expected justice.
Convinced betrayal had reached his own table, Baelenys ordered every member of the royal kitchens executed.
Princess Saera alone dared question him before the assembled court.
"My king," she asked, "will you punish years of loyal service for the deeds of one stranger?"
Prince Visenor pledged to hunt down every conspirator, swearing that even Essos itself would burn before those responsible escaped his wrath.
Rather than plead for mercy, he argued for reason.

A kingdom, he warned, could not afford to sacrifice loyal servants every time a single traitor slipped through its gates. If trust itself became disposable, the crown would soon find no one left worthy of it.
Only later that same week did the truth begin to emerge.

Prince Gaelor, a warrior far more comfortable with steel than politics, spent three bloody days personally questioning those connected to the kitchens. Under torture he uncovered a crucial detail: among the long-serving cooks had been a newly hired bald man, unknown to the rest of the staff.
When finally cornered, the suspect uttered only a single accusation.
Before another word could be spoken, Gaelor struck him down.
The alleged assassin died where he stood.
Only Gaelor and his sworn sword, Ser Saathos, heard the confession.
Whether it had been truth, a lie, or merely the desperate words of a dying man would never be known.
In killing him, Gaelor had silenced not only a traitor, but perhaps the only witness capable of proving who truly stood behind the poisoning.
Meanwhile, Ser Alester Dayne uncovered another secret.

A hidden letter revealed that Lady Rhaella had secretly warned her family beyond the kingdom's borders. Confronted with the evidence, the young woman broke into tears, insisting she had acted out of fear rather than treason.
Rather than expose her immediately, Jace and Saera chose a different path.
Together they informed Visenor in private. An uneasy agreement was reached: if Visenor delayed the coming war, Rhaella would be spared public disgrace.
For the first time since the poisoning, the king's children had chosen cooperation over rivalry.
The poisoning had accomplished far more than weakening a king.
Visenor began to doubt even his own siblings.
Gaelor abandoned patience in favor of impulse, earning a reputation for rashness where once he had simply been admired as a warrior.
Saera found herself carrying the burden of conscience within a family increasingly ruled by suspicion.
Jace, meanwhile, emerged as something far more dangerous than an ambitious prince.
He became a man who had begun to realize that nearly every warning he had given was proving true.
And somewhere within the walls of the Red Keep, if the dying assassin had spoken honestly, the greatest betrayal of all still wore the king's chain.

title: Chapter II: The Price of Trust
synopsis: A deception surrounding the assassin spirals into political tension, exposing fractures within the royal family.
content: If the hatching of dragons had marked the beginning of the realm's unrest, the feast that followed revealed something far more dangerous.
Some wore a chain of office.
Prince Gaelor was the first to move.

It was the twentieth day of the second moon, and the man who had confessed with his dying breath that the Hand had ordered the poisoning was already beyond questioning. Dead men told no tales. Yet the prince refused to let the trail end there. Hidden among the assassin's belongings was a scrap of parchment bearing the markings of the Iron Bank, account numbers and records of payments that no common cutthroat should ever possess.
But it was enough to weave a snare.

A sellsword from the south, broad-shouldered and dark of skin, was bought with gold and silence. His head was covered with a hood, chains clasped around his wrists, and he was cast into the dungeons as though he were the very poisoner who had tried to kill the king.
Let the realm believe the assassin still lived.
Then wait to see who came to finish him.
Jacaelon approved the deception at once.
Yet the prince trusted evidence more than instinct. Before any accusations could be whispered, he proposed another path.

While Gaelor and Visenor occupied the king and his Hand in the throne room, Jace slipped through the corridors with Princess Saera beside him. Together they entered the council chamber like ghosts beneath candlelight.
The desk of the Hand held enough secrets to drown a kingdom.
The handwriting upon the bank records belonged to another.
Not unless another hand had penned his crimes.
They found no answers before footsteps echoed through the halls.
Gaelor delayed the Hand with cups of wine and drunken laughter until the search was finished. It was enough.
The trap would have to suffice.
That evening the royal family gathered beneath golden chandeliers while servants poured wine as though the court had never smelled poison.
She had asked him only hours before whether she would still have been his wife had there been no letters.
Instead they had clung to one another like two souls already mourning a future neither believed would come.
"So," he asked. "I hear my children have been making plans."
Then Visenor spoke of delaying the war.
The room seemed to breathe easier.

His boots echoed across the stone floor as he strode toward the great hearth. The fire answered him with a sudden crack, sending sparks dancing into the air. Rage rolled from the king in silent waves. For a fleeting moment, it seemed as though the chamber itself had become the chest of some slumbering dragon, drawing slow, heavy breaths before unleashing fire upon the world.
Without warning he withdrew a folded letter from within his robes and flung it across the table.
It struck Visenor squarely in the face.
"What," the king asked, his voice colder than winter seas, "is this?"
They had not been keeping secrets from the king.

When Jace defended himself, saying he had first informed the heir because it was proper, because to go behind his brother's back would have sown discord within the royal family, the king's anger only deepened.
Baelenys had no patience left for such words.
He stripped Blackfyre from Visenor's hands before the entire court and placed the ancient sword in Gaelor's keeping.
Visenor stared at the empty place where Blackfyre had rested only moments before.
"Father… I never meant to deceive you. I only feared what might become of Rhaella. I thought..."
Baelenys crossed the distance between them with startling speed.
The crack of the slap echoed through the hall like the report of a whip.
Visenor collapsed onto the stone floor before anyone could move. Two bloodied teeth skipped across the flagstones, spinning to a halt beneath the long table.
The only sound was the faint crackling of the fire.
Jacaelon was the first to move.
He dropped to one knee beside his brother, slipping an arm beneath his shoulders to help him rise.
Visenor's mouth was filled with blood.
For the briefest moment, Jace forgot the intrigues, the lies, the rivalries, the crown itself.
All he saw was his brother.
Baelenys looked down upon them without remorse.
"Next time," the king said, his voice as cold as forged steel, "remember who it is you dare disappoint."
Little Princess Vhaemys buried her face against the table, unable to watch.
Only then did Jace look back at his father.
But something behind his violet eyes had changed.
As the Hand departed, the prince silently caught Ser Alester Dayne's eye.
Derrin Hightower descended into the dungeons exactly as Jace had predicted.
Then Ser Alester himself appeared, calm as still water.
For several long moments the Hand measured the two knights.
Yet no innocent man would have hurried to a prison cell before dawn.
That night Baelenys summoned Visenor alone.
The hall felt less like a throne room than a place of judgment.
"I did not love you. Love is for mothers and fools. I trusted you."
…or kill your own wife and earn your king's trust anew.
Visenor left without giving an answer.
Only after the doors closed did he begin to understand the man who had raised him.
He found Jace and Gaelor waiting.
"I must flee," he said. "Rhaella and I cannot stay."
Gaelor rejected the idea before the words had finished leaving his mouth.
The three princes withdrew to Gaelor's chambers.
"I have never seen a man as spineless as you."
He shoved the heir hard enough for him to stagger.
Only Visenor heard the whisper that followed.
When the performance was complete, Jace walked away without another word.
Later that night Visenor entered his brother's chambers in secret.
Jace looked at him for a long while.
Jace spoke not as a brother, but as a prince.

"If you cross the Narrow Sea, you become a claimant in exile. Your wife's family, our uncle, every enemy of this house will use your name. Whether willingly or in chains, you would become the spark that burns the Seven Kingdoms."
Ser Alester Dayne accepted the task without hesitation.
The escape began beneath a moon veiled by clouds.
Alester procured a humble wagon that looked fit only for merchants and beggars. He crossed forgotten passages beneath the castle, where damp stone swallowed every footstep.
One guard fell unconscious before he knew another man shared the corridor.
The prince descended with Rhaella carrying what little remained of their lives: coin, provisions, and hope.
The wagon rolled into the darkness.
Torches bloomed across the castle walls.
Before they departed, Jace gave Alester one final command.
"When you return, you were never escorting a prince."
"You went to Dorne to bring Lorenah Dayne to court."
"...that is the only story that ever happened."
Every sound beyond his chamber door became footsteps coming for him.
Every gust of wind became a raven bearing terrible news.
Three riderless dragons still remained within the castle.
And somewhere within the Red Keep, Jacaelon Targaryen finally understood a truth his father had known for years.
The hardest lies were never told to enemies.

title: Chapter III: The Weight of Loyalty
synopsis: As Prince Visenor vanishes, loyalties are tested across the realm, forcing princes, knights, and kings alike to bear the weight of their oaths.
content: The Red Keep had become quieter since Prince Visenor vanished.

The silence that settled over the castle did not belong to rest, but to uncertainty. Servants lowered their voices as they passed beneath vaulted corridors. Guards watched strangers a little longer than before. Even the ravens seemed reluctant to break the stillness, their cries swallowed by the cold morning air drifting in from Blackwater Bay.
No gate had recorded his departure. No rider had seen him upon the kingsroad. No fisherman claimed to have ferried him across the bay.
It was as if the castle itself had swallowed the heir to the Iron Throne.
Gaelor stood in the yard where the training dummies overlooked the sea.
A practice sword rested across his shoulders while squires hurried around him, pretending not to stare.
He had spent the better part of the morning searching every possibility in his mind.
The confession of the assassin still echoed inside his skull.
Only two men had heard those words.
Dead men could no longer speak.
Living men could choose not to.
Not because he trusted Derrin Hightower.
Because accusations without proof were merely sharper blades pointed inward.
If the Hand truly wished the assassin silenced...
The prisoner would remain exactly where everyone believed he was.
Gaelor himself would stand watch wearing the armor of an ordinary gaoler.
Elsewhere in the castle another prince carried a different burden.
Every sound beyond his chamber door had become footsteps.
Every gust of wind became the scrape of armor.
Every raven's cry sounded like terrible news carried across the sea.
The thought refused to leave him.
He had convinced himself the plan was necessary.
Little Princess Vhaemys ran through the corridor with all the determination a child could muster before nearly colliding with him.
He caught her instinctively before she could lose her balance.
Jace forced the corners of his mouth upward.
Her silver hair bounced as she tilted her head.
"I wanted him to tell me another story."
For a moment Jace could not answer.
He remembered the countless evenings Alester had spent entertaining the youngest princess while the court argued over wars and taxes beyond closed doors.
The knight had always found time.
Even when no one else had.
Vhaemys tugged gently at his sleeve.
The words escaped before he could stop them.
The little princess hugged him around the waist without another word.
For reasons Jace himself did not fully understand...
That hurt more than anything else.
By sunset the Great Hall had filled once again.
Conversation attempted to pretend nothing had changed.
King Baelenys sat motionless upon the raised dais.
His face had grown thinner since the poisoning.
The left side still drooped ever so slightly, lending every expression the appearance of restrained fury.
His eyes swept across the hall.
The king did not look away from it.
At last the great doors opened.
A man entered whom few recognized.
He wore neither white cloak nor polished plate.
His shoulders were broader than most doorways.
Age had not diminished him so much as compressed him into something harder.
Scars crossed his thick neck like faded ropes.
Curtass Whent bowed his head only slightly.
Jace felt the warmth leave his hands.
His thoughts found the knight immediately.
There had once been songs about him.
Not many men lived long enough to become songs.
Fewer still after leaving the Kingsguard.
No one truly tasted the food.
Jace barely noticed what rested upon his own plate.
His fingers tightened around his cup often enough that Saera eventually leaned toward him.
"The rest of you left this hall hours ago."
"I suppose I have much on my mind."
She watched him for another heartbeat.
Neither of them noticed Gaelor quietly leaving through another doorway.
Each sibling carried worries the others could not yet see.
Outside, the evening air smelled of salt and rain.
Cloudgazer waited upon the cliffs beyond the dragonpit, enormous wings folded against scales the color of storm clouds.
The dragon opened one great golden eye as Saera approached.
Saera stroked the dragon's snout before climbing into the saddle.
Moments later the dragon launched into the sky.
King's Landing fell away beneath them.
The Red Keep became no larger than a child's toy.
Ships drifted like leaves across Blackwater Bay.
For the first time in days...
Not because his worries had disappeared.
Only because, for a few fleeting moments...
Cloudgazer climbed until the cries of the city were carried away by the wind.

King's Landing shrank beneath them, its crooked streets dissolving into ribbons of stone and shadow. The Red Keep remained unmistakable, perched atop Aegon's High Hill like a crimson beast watching over its prey. From this height it seemed almost peaceful.
He rested one gloved hand against the saddle and looked toward the sea. Somewhere beyond the horizon lay Dorne. Somewhere beyond that endless stretch of blue rode Visenor, Rhaella, and Ser Alester.
Or perhaps they no longer rode at all.
"You've been staring at the ground since we took off."
Saera's voice barely rose above the wind.
Cloudgazer glided through a bank of clouds, leaving them wrapped in cool mist for only a heartbeat before emerging once more into fading sunlight.
"I've watched you since we were children," she continued quietly. "You only become this quiet when you're blaming yourself."
She looked toward the distant sea.
There was no humor in it.
"I spent years believing I could solve everything if I simply thought long enough."
The setting sun painted the sea with streaks of molten gold.
Below them lay an isolated hill of pale stone rising from dense woodland. No road reached its summit. No horse could climb its slopes.
The dragon landed with surprising grace, folding his wings while warm breath drifted across the grass.
Neither sibling spoke for a while.
Saera sat near the cliff's edge.
"When we were children," she said softly, "Father brought us here."
"He told us no castle was worth dying for."
They watched the sun descend together.
For the first time since Visenor vanished...
Neither of them spoke about politics.
The dungeon smelled of damp stone, rust, and old blood.
Gaelor hated every part of it.
His borrowed mail scratched against his neck. The plain helmet concealed most of his face, leaving him indistinguishable from the ordinary gaolers passing through the corridor.
The false prisoner remained inside the cell.
Chains clinking softly whenever he shifted.
He had played the part well.
Well enough that even some of the guards believed he truly was the poisoner.
Somewhere deeper in the cells, a prisoner coughed until the sound dissolved into painful wheezing.
Near midnight another guard appeared carrying a wooden tray.
Placed a bowl of stew beside the chained prisoner.
The corridor fell silent once more.
The silence slowly transformed into frustration.
If Derrin truly feared the prisoner...
Every passing minute chipped away at his certainty.
By dawn only one conclusion remained.
Either the Hand had already realized it was a trap...
...or he had never intended to spring it in the first place.
The wagon rolled across uneven ground while morning mist clung stubbornly to the trees.
Rhaella wrapped her cloak tighter around herself.
"...the realm becomes divided the moment we arrive."
"I won't be remembered as the prince who began a civil war because he was afraid."
Rhaella's voice became almost a whisper.
Years of service had taught him that the most dangerous road was often the quietest one.
His hand drifted toward his sword.
The forest became still enough that even the horses seemed reluctant to breathe.
Visenor released the breath he had been holding.
He had learned long ago that instincts rarely lied.
If the forest had been watching them...
The first rays of dawn touched the towers of the Red Keep.
None of those within its walls expected it to bring peace.
The bells of the Red Keep rang shortly after sunrise.

The sound rolled across King's Landing like distant thunder, drawing guards from their posts, stewards from their ledgers, and messengers toward the Tower of the Hand. Before the hour was over, ravens had begun taking flight from the rookery in every direction.
Every lord in the Seven Kingdoms would soon receive the same decree.
King Baelenys stood before the Iron Throne as the maester finished sealing the final scroll.
The old king had not slept.
"Prince Visenor Targaryen is to be found immediately."
asked one of the scribes cautiously.
Baelenys closed his eyes for only a heartbeat.
"No man is to raise steel against him unless there is no other choice."
Not long after, Gaelor was summoned to the throne room.
Only the king, the queen, Jace, and a handful of trusted attendants remained.
Baelenys studied his second son for several moments before speaking.
The words seemed to weigh upon him.
"You will become heir to the Iron Throne."
"I did not ask whether you wanted it."
A heavy silence settled between them.
The king's gaze drifted toward one of the tall windows overlooking Blackwater Bay.
"There are only two paths left to him."
Though he felt every word like another stone tied around his neck.
"Driftmark would be more appropriate than King's Landing."
He reached for a parchment already resting upon the table.
"The expenses can be drawn from the reparations."
"They will not pay for a royal celebration."
For the first time that morning...
A faint smile touched the king's face.
"My own treasury will bear the cost."
Once the matter of the wedding had been settled, Gaelor stepped forward again.
At once the room grew still.
The strange financial records discovered among the poisoner's belongings.
The failed attempt by the Hand to enter the dungeons the previous night.
"We did not wish to accuse your oldest friend without evidence."
"So we remained silent until we learned more."
The king placed several folded documents into his son's grasp.
"The Iron Bank leaves records where men leave lies."
"If the gold truly belongs to Derrin Hightower..."
The great doors shut behind them with a deep groan.
Baelenys remained motionless for several long moments.
His cane struck the steps beneath the Iron Throne.
The sound echoed through the chamber.
A nearby chair splintered beneath another blow.
A goblet crashed against the wall.
Wine ran across the stone like fresh blood.
The king's voice broke into a whisper.
Whether the fury belonged to betrayal...
Dust rose beneath the wheels of the wagon as Alester guided the horses along a narrower road.
The main highways had become dangerous.
They traveled instead through forgotten tracks that wound between rocky hills and sparse woodland.
Rhaella leaned against the side of the wagon.
"Do you think they've realized we're heading south?"
"I hope they think we're halfway to Essos."
This time there had been no mistake.
His hand rested upon Dawn's hilt.
A handful of rough-looking riders emerged between the trees.
The man glanced toward the wagon.
The first man urged his horse forward.
Only enough to reach the wagon.
Alester moved so quickly that Visenor almost missed it.
One hand seized the rider by the throat.
The body collapsed before anyone else understood what had happened.
The first outlaw fell without so much as a cry.
His body struck the earth with a dull thud, eyes still wide in surprise.
For the briefest instant, no one moved.
The remaining riders drew their swords almost as one. One spurred his horse straight at Alester while another circled toward the wagon.
"Alive if you can," one of them barked.
"No," another answered with a grin. "Dead pays just as well."
He stepped into the charging horse rather than away from it.
Valyrian steel met the rusted blade.
Before the rider could comprehend what had happened, the sword's pale edge swept upward beneath his arm.
The man toppled from the saddle.
The horse continued several paces before realizing its rider was gone.
Behind him another bandit rushed the wagon.
A heartbeat later Alester reached the attacker first.
The pommel of the knight's sword crashed into the man's temple.
He collapsed unconscious before his sword ever left its sheath.
Not a knight who moved like water over polished stone.
Steel flashed beneath the morning sun.
Alester gave ground for the first time.
Because every step drew the fight farther from Visenor and Rhaella.
The sword slipped beneath his guard.
A narrow crimson line appeared across the man's throat.
The fourth outlaw screamed and rushed forward.
He died before finishing the scream.
He stared at the corpses surrounding him.
By the time the sun reached its height, the abandoned wagon had become more burden than blessing.
Its broken wheel protested every stone.
Its tracks marked their course too clearly.
Alester spoke with the certainty of a man who had already made the decision hours earlier.
They transferred what supplies they could onto horseback.
Visenor looked back once before mounting.
"No one chases footprints they can't follow."
Then they rode south once more.
Weeks later, as the third moon began, the sea welcomed House Targaryen to Driftmark.
Salt carried on the wind long before the island came into view.
The towers of High Tide rose from the cliffs like pale fingers reaching toward a grey sky.
Ships bearing the banners of Velaryon and Targaryen crowded the harbor.
The wedding had drawn half the nobility of the eastern coast.
Musicians filled the halls before the royal family had even crossed the gates.
Servants hurried between kitchens and courtyards carrying silver trays heavy with roasted meats, fresh bread, and Arbor wines.
Everything had been prepared for celebration.
No one spoke openly of the prince who should have stood among them.
Yet every conversation eventually found its way back to him.
Whispers flowed more freely than wine.
Gaelor stood alone for a moment before the ceremony began.
His gaze wandered toward the sea.
He wondered whether Visenor still breathed.
Whether Jace had uncovered anything more about the poisoning.
Whether today's smiles would still exist by nightfall.
She wore silver embroidered upon sea-green silk.
No jewels beyond a simple pearl necklace.
"...I'd think you didn't understand what today means."
For the first time that morning...
The ceremony passed beneath the blessing of the Seven.
The septon's voice echoed through the hall.
When it ended, applause filled High Tide.
The realm remembered how to celebrate.
Even King Baelenys allowed himself a faint smile as lords raised goblets in honor of the newly married couple.
Jace watched it all from his place further down the table.
His thoughts had not left the road to Dorne.
The doors to the hall opened.
His eyes stopped at the seal.
The prince went outside and broke the wax.
His eyes moved quickly across the page.
He read the same lines again.
Everything led back to one man.
Jace went back into the room and stepped beside the king.
The old king never changed expression.
He spoke just loudly enough for Jace alone.
No one beyond the royal family understood that, in the space of a single whispered conversation...
The fate of the Hand of the King had already been sealed.
The feast carried on as though nothing had happened.

Music echoed beneath the timbered ceilings. Lords drank. Ladies laughed behind jeweled hands. Servants hurried between tables with platters of roasted swan, lamprey pie, buttered turnips, and sweet Arbor wines.
King Baelenys smiled for the first time that day.

Naella sat beside Gaelor, her cheeks still flushed from the ceremony. Whenever another lord or lady approached to offer congratulations, the king insisted they toast the newlyweds before speaking of anything else. For a little while, war, succession, and missing princes ceased to exist.
Gaelor played his part well. Drank only enough to avoid suspicion. Thanked every guest who approached. Yet beneath the smiles his thoughts never strayed far from Derrin Hightower.
If the Hand truly stood behind everything...
...then every passing moment gave him another chance to erase the truth.
Across the hall, Jace barely touched his wine.
The confirmation resting inside his sleeve like a blade pressed against his ribs.
He scarcely noticed when someone stopped beside him.
A silver goblet entered his view.
Lord Derrin Hightower stood over him, smiling with the effortless warmth that had earned him the trust of half the realm.
"One day," he said, "we shall celebrate a wedding just as grand as this one."
Jace stared at him for a heartbeat.
Derrin's smile remained, though something behind his eyes shifted ever so slightly.
The old Hand studied the prince's face.
For just a fleeting instant, unease crept into the old man's chest.
He could not have said why.
Only that instinct, sharpened by decades at court, whispered that something invisible had changed.
Still smiling, he raised his goblet once more.
Derrin lingered only another moment before disappearing back into the sea of nobles.
Jace watched him until he vanished.
The feast continued deep into the night.
One by one the guests drifted away toward their chambers.
At last the castle grew quiet.
Gaelor closed the door behind him.
Naella smiled as candlelight danced across the room.
"You've looked like a man carrying the weight of the realm."
She rested a hand against his chest.
The words loosened something inside him.
Then with all the restraint he had carried throughout the day finally slipping away.
Outside, waves crashed against Driftmark's cliffs.
Inside, the newlyweds disappeared into the warmth of their chamber while the realm, for a few precious hours, pretended peace still existed.
Elsewhere in the castle, another prince walked alone.
Jace moved silently through empty corridors lit only by dying torches.
Every step echoed against ancient stone.
At the end of one passage stood a lone guard outside the Hand's chambers.
"The chambers of Prince Gaelor and Princess Naella require additional security."
He bowed quickly before hurrying away.
Jace waited until the footsteps faded entirely.
Only then did he turn toward the carved oak door.
Lord Derrin Hightower sat alone beside the fire.
He had not changed for bed.
Papers still covered the table before him.
A dagger rested beside his hand.
Jace closed the door behind him.
"I cannot speak to His Grace yet."
Outside, somewhere beyond the walls, waves crashed endlessly against the rocks below.
Something in his voice caused the old man to look up properly.
"I need you to come with me."
Derrin's fingers rested upon the table.
For the briefest instant they shifted toward the dagger.
Then folded his hands before him and walked toward the door.
As they crossed the corridor, the old man muttered something beneath his breath.
"I simply fail to understand why this cannot wait until morning."
"You are the Hand of the King."
"Surely matters concerning the attempted murder of the king take precedence over travel arrangements."
They descended into the sleeping castle.
Past silent courtyards washed silver beneath the moon.
He turned toward one of the smaller stone buildings bordering the court.
The chamber beyond had long since been abandoned.
A single lantern burned dimly upon the wall.
Jace closed the door behind them.
Neither man spoke for several moments.
Only then did Jace finally turn to face him.
"I believe we've found the men responsible for poisoning my father."
Derrin Hightower did not answer at once.
The lantern between them hissed softly, its flame bending in the draft that slipped beneath the old door.
"I've obtained a document," Jace continued. "One important enough to make everything else fall into place."
The old Hand's expression remained unreadable.
He counted the pieces one by one.
"They may all be part of the same design."
He kept his face carefully still.
Something eased inside the old man.
A slight lowering of the shoulders.
He continued before the silence could recover.
"And they appear to belong to Prince Gaelor."
The words hung heavily in the room.
Jace spoke them as though he believed every one.
"He removed his brother from the succession."
"...he stands one step from inheriting the Iron Throne."
"Do you have anything to say about that document, Lord Hand?"
The old man remained perfectly still.
Yet something behind his eyes betrayed recognition.
"I believe there is still a future from which we both walk away satisfied."
The old Hand studied him for what felt like an eternity.
Jace felt certainty settle inside him like cold iron.
He forced himself not to react.
"I asked whether I could trust you."
"And whether we can leave this room with both of us satisfied."
For the first time that evening...
Jace met his eyes without blinking.
"There is no need for anyone else to know the details."
"You served beside my father for decades."
"Why would you do such a thing?"
His eyes drifted toward the floor.
"...is no longer moving in the right direction."
"But love alone cannot preserve a dynasty."
"If your father could see what lay ahead..."
The words struck Jace like insults wrapped in silk.
The man who had almost murdered his king dared speak of loyalty.
Instead he asked the question that mattered most.
The old Hand closed his eyes briefly.
"He saw the same future I did."
He sounded as though he were reciting prayer rather than confession.
"Do not concern yourself. I will deal with Gaelor."
Relief spread openly across Derrin's face.
"...you may need to deal with him."
The old Hand answered without hesitation.
"We should never again be seen discussing this."
"If we truly intend to change anything."
"I am glad we understand one another, Prince Jacaelon."
Jace stepped aside from the door.
Placed one hand upon the latch.
He took a single step forward.
The prince's sword moved faster than thought.
The blade punched cleanly through the side of the old man's neck.
His breath vanished in a wet gasp.
His hands flew instinctively to the wound.
His mouth worked desperately, trying to form words that would never come.
Then collapsed heavily onto the cold stone floor.
Only the lantern continued to burn.
Jace watched until the last tremor left the old man's body.
He wiped the dark blood from his sword with the hem of his own cloak.
Jace did not linger over the corpse.
The blood had already begun to cool, thickening in the grooves of the stone floor. The lantern hissed softly above him, its flame steady as if nothing in the room had changed at all.
He wiped his sword once more, slower this time, then slid it back into its sheath. Only then did he step over the dead man and leave the chamber.

He moved through the sleeping heart of the castle without being stopped, past carved arches and torchlit alcoves, past tapestries that watched him like mute witnesses. By the time he reached the royal wing, his face had already become something blank and practiced.

Inside, Queen Jaery slept in a chair by the hearth, a half-burned candle beside her and a book resting open on her lap. The fire had died low. Her head tilted slightly, her hair falling over one shoulder.
She did not wake as he passed.
King Baelenys sat alone at a heavy table beneath a half-lit sconce. No crown. No attendants. Just parchment, ink, and the weight of a man who had stopped pretending the night would bring rest.
His eyes lifted as Jace entered.
Jace stopped a few paces from the table.

He spoke carefully at first, then with growing precision, as if laying out a ledger rather than a murder. The trap. The conversation. Derrin’s hesitation, his confession, the name of Highgarden, the belief that it was all for the realm.
He did not omit the moment the old man broke.
Nor the moment he did not.
Without even the courtesy of surprise.
When Jace finished, silence filled the room like water rising in a sealed chamber.
Jace held the silence a moment longer, as if expecting it to change shape.
Driftmark did not sleep the way castles slept.
Driftmark was always half-awake, its cliffs breathing salt wind into stone corridors even in the dead of night.
Jace walked alone until he reached the edge of the cliffs.
Below him, the sea struck the rocks in slow, patient violence.
He returned for the body before dawn.
It took effort to move it. More than he expected. Not because of weight alone, but because of what it represented now that the words had been spoken out loud.
He bound stones to the corpse with rope taken from a dockside storehouse. Simple work. Careful work. Work that left no room for thought.
When he was done, he dragged the body to the cliff’s edge.
The sea took it without hesitation.
No sound lingered in any meaningful way. Only wind. Only waves. Only the indifferent rhythm of water that had swallowed worse things than men.
Jace watched until nothing remained but foam.
By morning, Driftmark was alive again.
Servants shouted orders. Horses were saddled. Ships prepared to receive passengers. The wedding had ended, and with it the illusion that anything had been resolved.
Jace moved through the chaos until he found Gaelor.
His brother was already dressed for travel.
“I spoke with the King about the Hand matter.”
“I have taken full control of the investigation.”
“My only request is that you do not question this again until things calm down.”
“There was a guard outside the Hand’s chambers last night.”
Did not ask anything at all.
The man was easy to find.
A young guard from Driftmark’s garrison. The kind who still believed service meant honor rather than survival. He straightened immediately when Gaelor approached.
“You will drink with me,” Gaelor said.
“That is why it matters,” Gaelor interrupted.
At first, the man was careful. Then less careful. Then honest in the way only drunk men become honest, when caution finally drowns beneath wine and attention.
Gaelor listened. Smiled when appropriate. Refilled his cup often enough that the guard stopped counting his own.
Color drained from the man’s face.
“I could not. I would not dare. I am not—”
“How many times in your life will you get to cross blades with a prince?”
They moved to a quieter yard.
At first it looked like nothing more than practice. Steel clashing against steel. Controlled movements. Measured steps. The guard sweating, trying to keep up.
The guard collapsed before he understood what had happened.
Gaelor stood over him for a moment, breathing evenly.
Then he dragged the body to the cliffs.
And let the sea take it.
By the middle of the third moon, far to the south, the road narrowed again.
The world there was older. Quieter. More willing to kill without witnesses.
Visenor rode in silence for most of the day. Rhaella beside him, her hood drawn low. Alester ahead, always ahead, as if distance alone could absorb danger.
“The Dornish never loved us. Never loved House Dayne.”
Alester continued anyway, voice roughened by memory.
“I grew up under every kind of pressure you can imagine. Economic, physical, and psychological.”
“My mother is a Karstark,” he added. “From the North. My parents’ marriage was purely political... at first.”
“The only reason I am alive,” Alester said, “is King Baelenys. And I am more grateful to His Grace than you might think.”
That name lingered in the air like iron.
For a moment, even the wind seemed to hesitate.
His hand went to his sword.
And one who did not belong with them.
No white cloak. No oath. No honor-marked armor. Only a man shaped by violence and history.
“There is no prince here,” Alester said.
Visenor did not move. Rhaella did not speak.
“I'm afraid that's not possible, shiny boy.”
Alester understood there would be no passing.
Rhaella and Visenor vanished into the road ahead, swallowed by dust and trees.
And the world broke into violence.
Back in King’s Landing, the throne room was being prepared.
The King had not yet sat on his throne.
Queen Jaery stood near the Iron Throne, watching servants move like shadows across stone. When Jace entered, she turned immediately.
She stepped forward and placed both hands on his face.
“You think your father does not see you.”
And pinned the Hand’s badge onto his chest.
For a moment, everything inside him collapsed inward.
And still he tried to hold it in.
But the realm did not ask him to be strong.
So he stood there, unable to move, as the weight of iron and meaning settled onto his chest, and his eyes finally began to blur.

title: Chapter IV: The Broken Knight
synopsis: As Jacaelon exerts his new authority in a restless court, the King plots a ruthless vengeance. Beyond the capital, a desperate fight for survival tests the limits of loyalty.
content: By the middle of the third moon, the damp stones of the rural sept smelled of old incense and stale bread. Visenor pulled his hood lower as they moved deeper into the sanctuary, the heavy wooden doors sealing out the biting wind of the kingsroad. A quiet sisterhood of the Faith moved like grey ghosts through the arched halls, paying the newcomers no mind as they offered them a meager supper of broth and hard cheese.
Rhaella’s face was pale in the flickering candlelight. She pushed her wooden bowl away, her stomach churning. "Ser Dayne," she murmured, her voice trembling. "Is he coming?"
"I would have turned back for him," Visenor said, his voice low but firm, "but I could not risk you. He knows where to find us."
Rhaella dry-heaved slightly, clutching her cloak. "I just need to sleep," she whispered.

Visenor nodded, scanning the cavernous room where the nuns knelt in silent prayer. "We spend the night here. We’ll take the watches in turns." The sisters of the Faith walked past them, their eyes cast downward, completely indifferent to the dragons hiding in their midst.

Back in the capital, weeks had passed since the blood spilled at Driftmark. Jace had barely managed a short nap when the summons came. The heavy oak doors of the royal apartments groaned as he and Gaelor walked the corridors toward the throne room.
Gaelor’s eyes fell to Jace’s chest, catching the glint of the Hand’s pin. He stopped. "Jace, what is that? What happened to the Hand?"
Jace didn't break his stride, adjusting the collar of his doublet. "Did I not tell you to stop questioning things?" he said coldly, pushing past the guards and entering the Great Hall.
The King sat at a heavy wooden table near the base of the Iron Throne, cracking walnuts with one hand, his thick fingers crushing the shells with brutal, rhythmic snaps. A Kingsguard stood nearby.
"Did you send the word to Oldtown?" the King asked the Maester, his voice rumbling like distant thunder.
"Send another raven," the King commanded, tossing a shattered walnut shell onto a silver platter. "Tell Baelor Targaryen to return to King's Landing immediately."
*Uncle Baelor.* The scholar who had forsaken his rights for a life buried in books at the Citadel. His name carried a quiet weight in Oldtown, a weight they now desperately needed.

The King looked up, his sharp gaze locking onto Jace. "You will remain Hand. I am not calling my uncle to replace you. But in these times, especially with the troubles we may soon face from the Tyrells, having him here is vital."
Gaelor stepped forward, unable to help himself. "Father, what happened to the Hand?"

The King glanced at Jace, deferring the answer. Jace’s expression remained an unreadable mask of Valyrian stone. "It is reasonable, Your Grace," Jace said smoothly, entirely dismissing his brother's question.
"If there is something I must tell you, I will," the King said to Gaelor. "But if the Hand has seen fit not to speak of it today, then that is how it remains."
Gaelor’s jaw tightened. "I was part of this investigation too."
Jace turned, his eyes narrowing. "Did His Grace stutter? You have done your part. Leave the rest to us."
"It was I who led this pursuit! It is my right to know!" Gaelor snapped back.
"Gaelor!" The King’s roar echoed off the cavernous ceiling of the throne room. The crushing of the walnuts ceased.
Before the silence could settle, the heavy doors opened again. Princess Saera walked in, the rustle of her skirts the only sound in the tense hall.
"Saera," the King said, his tone shifting from fury to gravelly command. "You will take your dragon and ride North. Alone."

"Lord Stark died in his bed while we were drinking and feasting at Driftmark," the King interrupted. "I never much cared for the man, but his father helped mine in ways that matter. You will go and offer our condolences. They favor you up there. Go, be their guest, and return."
Saera gave a slow, obedient nod. Her violet eyes flicked to Jace's chest, lingering on the Hand’s pin for a fraction of a second. She said nothing, turned on her heel, and left the hall.
The King waved a dismissive hand toward his eldest son. "We have matters to discuss with our hand Jacaelon. Come by my chambers tonight, Gaelor."

The ale in the cup was sour, but Gaelor drank it like water. He slammed the tankard down on the scarred wooden table of the dim tavern. Saathos sat across from him, watching the prince's seething anger with mild amusement.
"We were on this from the very beginning," Gaelor hissed, wiping foam from his mouth. "And they tell me nothing."
"The past two months have been a heavy burden for our King, you know this best, my prince," Saathos replied, swirling his own cup. "If you ask me, your brother is jealous of you."
Gaelor scoffed, a bitter sound. "Jace has no desire to rule. But he is a fucking prick."
"You had no desire to rule either," Saathos pointed out gently. "Yet here we are."
They drank in sullen silence for a while. Then, Gaelor felt a hand on his back. It was small, warm, and sweet-smelling. He turned to see a woman with soft eyes and painted lips smiling down at him.
"I could sweeten your evening, my lord," she purred.
The wine was swimming in Gaelor's head, numbing his pride. He didn't say no.

Upstairs, the mattress was stuffed with cheap straw, but the woman was skilled. Two rounds left him breathless and sweating in the damp chill of the room. As he tied his breeches, she smiled up from the tangled sheets.
"I am honored, my prince," she whispered. "In fact, this one is on me."
Gaelor tossed a silver stag onto the nightstand anyway, his gaze turning hard. "Keep this between us. If anyone hears of this, I will find you."
The woman laughed, a throaty, pleasant sound. "Of course, my prince. Though I would very much like for you to find me again."
Despite his foul mood, Gaelor chuckled as he slipped out into the night.
"What do you intend to do about the Tyrells, my King?" Jace asked, standing beside the massive, jagged swords of the Iron Throne.
The King leaned back. "What do you think we should do?"

"Such insolence cannot go unpunished," Jace said, his voice cold and precise. "We must show them that the Targaryens stand tall. The Crown must send a message. No one should ever dare attempt such a thing again."

The King nodded slowly. "I agree. I have tried to teach you and your siblings this. The Red Keep is not a brothel, nor a lovehouse. I am waiting for the official ledgers from the Iron Bank of Braavos. Derrin’s accounts, his meetings, the transactions. Once Baelor arrives, we will strike."
"It is reasonable, Your Grace," Jace said. "Flowers and bugs cannot stand against dragons."

Before they could speak further, a Kingsguard announced Queen Jaery. She entered with a flurry of silk, immediately demanding the King’s attention regarding wedding gifts for the Velaryons and Gaelor.
The King let out a heavy sigh, rubbing his temples. "We have an audience with the smallfolk now," he grumbled, looking toward Jace for salvation.
Jace stepped forward. "I am here, Your Grace."
The King nodded gratefully, but then his eyes narrowed as he looked around the hall. "By the way, where is your Kingsguard?" he asked, referring to Ser Alester.
"He went to escort Princess Dayne, who will be our guest, Your Grace," Jace lied smoothly.
The King’s expression soured. "We will speak of this," he muttered, turning and leaving the hall with the Queen.

Left alone to rule, Hand of the King Jacaelon Targaryen clasped his hands in front of him, leaned coolly against the iron armrest of the throne, and signaled for the doors to be opened to the complaining masses.
The forest floor was slick with wet leaves. Curtass Whent spat a glob of phlegm onto the dirt and grinned at the Kingsguard trapped before him.

"You know what the problem with the Kingsguard is?" Whent taunted, tossing his mace from hand to hand. "They pass over fixers—real men like me—and hire prancing pretty boys who spend their mornings brushing their hair."
Ser Alester Dayne said nothing. He kept his breathing steady, his eyes tracking Whent and the five sellswords fanning out to surround him.

Alester was a blur of white cloth and steel. He realized quickly there was no open retreat, so he used the terrain, his footwork impeccable. He fell back toward the thicket, disarming one man with a brutal twist of his wrist, only to be forced to abandon the kill as three more pressed him. The disarmed man scrambled to pick up a fallen blade and rejoined the fray.

Alester backed into the dense trees, using the trunks to cover his flanks. Whent came at him like a rabid bear, a broadsword in one hand and a heavy spiked mace in the other. Alester parried the sword, but the mace caught his pauldron. The steel crunched, sending a shockwave of pain down his arm. The distraction cost him; a sellsword’s dagger slipped past his guard, slicing his ribcage.
Alester rolled underneath a wild swing, his blade flashing out to hamstring two of the sellswords in one sweeping arc. They howled, collapsing into the mud, clutching their bleeding legs.
Alester backed his shoulders against a thick oak, his chest heaving, his breath pluming in the cold air.

Whent chuckled, resting his mace on his shoulder. "You're not walking out of here, pretty boy. Tell us where Visenor is. We won't even ask who hired you. We'll forget this ever happened, and you can run along."
Alester couldn't have answered even if he wanted to; he was fighting too hard for air. Whent didn't wait. He roared and charged.

As the men swarmed him, Alester grabbed the closest sellsword, driving his longsword straight through the man's gut. The blade burst through the man’s spine. Alester left it there, using the dying man as a fleshy shield to absorb the frantic dagger thrusts of his comrades. With a grunt of effort, Alester shoved the corpse directly into Whent, sending the brute stumbling back.
In the chaos, Alester drew his dagger and stepped inside the guard of the next man, severing his head from his shoulders in a spray of hot crimson.

But Whent shoved the corpse aside and swung his mace in a brutal upward arc. It slammed into Alester’s gut. The light armor shattered. Alester’s vision went white. Blood fountained from between his teeth, and he collapsed into the mud.

He dragged himself backward, crawling until he hit the oak tree again. He barely had time to pull himself up before a sellsword lunged at his throat. Alester caught the man's wrist, parrying the thrust, and ruthlessly threw him forward into the path of another charging mercenary, plunging his own dagger into the man's back as he fell.

One of the hamstrung men tried to limp toward him, eyes filled with murder. Alester sidestepped the clumsy swing, grabbed the man's head, and twisted savagely. The neck snapped with a sickening *crack*.
Whent stood over his remaining, wounded man. "You fucking useless cunt," Whent spat, and drove his sword through his own man's neck.
Whent turned his manic eyes to the battered, bloody Kingsguard. "Come on then. Let's see your knighthood."
They clashed. For fifteen seconds, it was a beautiful, desperate dance of steel. But Alester was losing blood fast. He feinted, turned, and broke into a sprint toward his tethered horse.
"Coward!" Whent roared, hurling his heavy mace like a stone.
Alester tried to dodge, but the spiked iron grazed his ear, tearing flesh and sending him crashing face-first into the dirt.

Before he could rise, Whent was on top of him. The brute’s fists rained down, heavy as anvils, smashing into Alester’s face, turning his handsome features into a bruised, bloody pulp. Whent laughed, his fingers digging into Alester’s eye sockets, trying to gouge them out.
Through the blinding pain, Alester snapped his jaw forward, catching one of Whent’s thick fingers between his teeth. He bit down with the force of a dying wolf. Bone crunched.
Whent shrieked, laughing and screaming in agony as he pulled his mangled, spurting stump away.

Alester spat the finger out and crawled frantically toward his horse. His blood-slicked fingers finally grasped the stirrup, but Whent slammed a heavy knee into his spine, pinning him flat to the earth.
"Maybe I shouldn't kill you right away," Whent panted, his breath reeking of rotten teeth and copper. "Maybe I should get some use out of you first, pretty boy."
Whent reached for the hilt of his sword to finish the job.
With the last ounce of strength in his shattered body, Alester twisted, sprang upward, and grabbed the horse's testicles. He squeezed with bone-crushing force.
The warhorse shrieked in terror and pain. It bucked violently, lashing out with its heavy, iron-shod hind hooves.

The kick caught Whent squarely under the jaw. His neck snapped backward with the sound of a breaking branch. Blood sprayed from his mouth as he crumpled to the ground, dead before he even realized what had happened.

Alester lay in the mud for a long moment, staring at the canopy of trees. He let out a ragged, bubbling sigh. Slowly, agonizingly, he wiped the mud from his face, sheathed his sword, and dragged his broken body up the side of the terrified horse. He draped himself over the saddle, struck the horse’s flank, and surrendered to the blackness.
"When you have your own child," little Vhaemys said, her face deadly serious, "I won't be the youngest anymore. I won't be the most loved. Will I?"
Gaelor smiled, kneeling to be eye-level with the girl. "Your place is always special, Vhaemys. Always."

Vhaemys puffed out her chest with childish pride. "Anyway, trust is more important than love. I am your blood. You'll trust me more." He paused, her eyes widening. "I saw you in my dream. You were riding a dragon."
She began to prattle on about animals. Maela, who was sitting near the hearth, looked over. "Do you always dream of animals, sweetling? Do you only dream of Gaelor among our family?"
"Yes," Vhaemys nodded eagerly. "Oh, and I saw a stag. It was huge. I was so scared."
Maela smiled proudly, a glint in her Baratheon eyes. "Our prince knows well, sweetling. No stag could ever scare us."
Gaelor ruffled the girl's hair, feeling a strange mix of affection and unease, before turning to head to the throne room.
Jace rubbed his eyes. He was bone-tired. The smallfolk seemed to drone on endlessly—complaints of dragons roasting their sheep, the exorbitant taxes, the muddy roads.
"Brother!" Gaelor called out as he swaggered into the throne room.
Queen Jaery walked in right behind him, catching his arm. "Are you just arriving, my son?" She took his hand, and they walked together toward the royal seats.
Gaelor grinned. "Doesn't the Hand's pin suit my brother perfectly?"

"As our King sees fit," Jaery replied coolly. "As the pin suits him, the crown will suit you. You are the heir. Do not let small things ruin your joy." She stopped and sniffed the air near him. Her eyes hardened. "You smell of cheap wine."
"I was just... spending some time with Saathos," Gaelor deflected.
"A new heir wandering the streets of King's Landing until dawn is not a welcome sight," Jaery chided softly but dangerously.
Gaelor scowled and muttered an excuse, leaving to bathe.
Jace watched him go, his lip curling. "Why did he even bother coming in just to leave?"
"He is your brother, Jace," Jaery said, taking her seat. "And most of all, he is the heir to the throne."

Jaery shot him a look that could have frozen the Narrow Sea. "We have listened to these people for two hours," she said, changing the subject. "They are starving. We need to get into Baelenys's head. Yes, the Tyrells must be punished, but the King must extract a heavy economic toll from them to replenish the realm."
Before Jace could reply, the heavy doors groaned open. The royal guards struck the floor with their halberds.
"Lord Hand," the herald announced. "As arranged, Lord Baratheon has arrived for his audience."

The night air was freezing outside the sept. Visenor crouched in the shadows as he heard the slow, rhythmic clopping of hooves. A solitary horse ambled into the courtyard. Slumped over its saddle was a man.
The horse stopped to graze, and the man slid off the saddle, crashing heavily into the bushes.

Visenor rushed forward, his heart hammering against his ribs. It was Alester. His face was a ruin of purple bruises and crusted blood, his armor dented and slashed. Visenor hoisted the knight over his shoulder, ignoring the strain, and dragged him inside, yelling for the silent sisters to bring bandages and boiling water.
Rhaella stood trembling by the altar. "Is it Ser Dayne?"
"Yes," Visenor grunted, laying him on a cot. "But he is in a bad way."
Alester’s eyes fluttered open. Only one eye could see; the other was swollen shut. "My prince," he rasped, blood bubbling on his lips. "I hope you kept your hood up getting here."
Visenor managed a tight smile. "We were hidden the whole way." He gently placed a hand on Alester’s shoulder.
Alester winced, his breath catching. "The worst is behind us, my prince. It will be easier to move from here."
"What happened to the men pursuing us?"
A ghostly smirk touched Alester’s ruined lips. "They were friendly lads. Gave up quick."
Rhaella stepped forward, tears pooling in her eyes. "Ser Dayne, you proved yourself a true hero today. The realm will not forget this."
Visenor looked down at the battered knight. "I certainly won't."

"Father," Gaelor said, leaning over the table in the King's solar. "The smallfolk are in a dire state. If this goes on, we'll be dealing with riots that could cripple us. Driftmark is ours now, my voice holds weight there. Let us take the Stepstones. We crush the pirates, ease the tax burden on Westeros with the spoils, and gain sympathy in Essos to strengthen our claim."
The King paused his reading and looked up at his son. "Are *you* going to take the Stepstones?" he asked. It wasn't a mockery; it was a genuine question of his son's resolve.
"Yes, Father," Gaelor said, puffing his chest.

The King stared at him for a long moment. "Let us handle the mess currently over our heads first. We will discuss this in the council." As Gaelor turned to leave, the King raised a single index finger, pointing it at his son's back—an enigmatic gesture that left Gaelor confused.

*(It had been a dark week for the Crown. In the dragonpits, tragedy had struck Visenor’s clutch. Two of his three young dragons had succumbed to grotesque birth defects, dying screaming in the dark. By royal decree, the sole surviving hatchling had been gifted to Maela.)*

Far to the North, the cold was absolute, a physical weight that pressed against the lungs. Princess Saera walked through the snow-draped courtyards of Winterfell, pulling her heavy furs tighter around her neck. Beside her walked Rickard Stark, the new Lord of Winterfell, mourning his father.

"Winterfell is beautiful," Saera said softly, her breath pluming in the icy air. "This is my first time here. My brothers love the south, especially Jacaelon. But I think I prefer this. It is quieter... less noisy."

Lord Rickard kept his hands clasped behind his back, his face solemn. "I would not wish to overstep, Princess, but I only wish you could see this place in all its true glory, rather than draped in this sorrow."

"I see it perfectly well," Saera replied, watching a snowflake melt on her gloved hand. "To see the snow fall with these eyes is a gift. I wish I could come here more often... and I wish it were not your father's passing that brought me."
Rickard stopped and looked at her, his grey eyes intense. "Frankly, Princess, we would like to see you here more often as well."
Saera blinked, her mind racing. Was this a crude attempt at proposing marriage so soon after a death? "Perhaps you truly are overstepping now, my lord," she said with a guarded smile.

"No, it is not like that," Rickard said earnestly. "Our King and the North have never had bad blood. Perhaps things have been tense recently, but His Grace knows my grandfather fought beside him. My father and grandfather bled against your uncle for the Crown. I believe the realm would like to see more of you. When people look up, they want to see someone like you."
Saera looked up at the towering grey walls of the keep. "I don't know what to say to that. Such a thing cannot be."

"But it suits you," Rickard said quietly. They stood in the falling snow, a long, unspoken understanding passing between them. Finally, Rickard gestured toward the horizon. "Come. Let me show you the Wall."
The massive oak doors of the Great Hall slammed open. "Lord Steffon of House Baratheon!" the herald bellowed.

The young lord marched in. He was barely seventeen, but he possessed the sheer, mountainous bulk of his lineage. A massive greatsword was strapped to his back, and his clothes were caked in mud and travel dust. Known as the Young Stag, he had already carved a bloody reputation in tourneys and duels. He stood before Jace, his stance wide, aggressive, and entirely devoid of respect.
Queen Jaery offered a patronizing smile. "Young Lord Baratheon, did they not teach you courtesy where you hail from? When you stand before a Prince or the Hand of the King, you take the knee."
Steffon set his jaw. "A Baratheon kneels when he feels safe."
"How do you mean?" Jaery asked, her smile hardening.

"The roads to King's Landing are crawling with bandits," Steffon spat. "We fought our way here. You nearly lost two Baratheon lords on your very highways. But we are used to it. The roads are hungry, and so is House Baratheon."

Steffon turned his glare to Jace. "Lord Hand, years ago, my house fought side-by-side with your father. We paid that debt in blood without blinking. Now, we demand a portion of these new taxes so that *you* can repay *your* debt to us."

Steffon then looked at the Queen. "Your Grace, I do not mean to belittle the Dragon. But respect must go both ways. Before the dragons came, the Stag was here. We all took the knee, but our houses are starving, and you are none too pleased with the unrest either. Winter is coming. This needs a solution."

Jace remained leaning against the Iron Throne, entirely unbothered. "Ser Steffon," he began, his voice dangerously soft. "Out of respect for your ailing father, I will handle this conversation with more gentleness than it deserves. Since when does *any* house demand things of the Targaryens? You come here to beg a boon, nothing more."

Jace pushed himself off the throne, taking a step down the dais. "We are well aware of the troubles, and we are working tirelessly. You have no idea what goes on within the walls of the Red Keep. We act for the good of all Seven Kingdoms."
"Why should any of that matter to us?" Steffon scoffed.

"Exactly," Jace snapped, his voice cracking like a whip. "None of it concerns you. The Crown owes a debt to no man. To even suggest such a thing is, to put it mildly, treason. The King does not serve. The King grants. *You* serve."

Jace’s eyes locked onto the boy's. "Our gratitude to your father is boundless. I understand what you are fumbling to say, and I will discuss it with His Grace. I chalk this insolence up to your youth, and out of respect for Lord Baratheon, I will pretend this conversation never happened. If you have no further business... enjoy your stay."
Steffon’s face turned the color of a bruised plum. His fists clenched so tight his knuckles turned white.

"The young stag is still too young, it seems," Queen Jaery noted with a musical laugh. A few courtiers tittered in the shadows. "If you meant to say these things to the King, consider the message delivered."

"Your Grace," Steffon choked out, bowing stiffly. He spun on his heel and stormed out of the hall, his boots echoing loudly on the marble. As he passed the guards, Jace heard him mutter, "Fucking prick."
Before leaving the keep, Steffon paused to ask a guard where Ser Alester Dayne was, itching to wash his humiliation away with a duel.
Morning light filtered through the stained glass of the sept. Ser Alester struggled to push himself up in his cot, his ribs screaming in protest.
"My prince," Alester croaked, looking at Visenor. "Let us do this. I am in no condition to protect you right now. Go on ahead. I will find you when I can ride."
Rhaella stepped forward, shaking her head. "No. We shouldn't do that."
"We shouldn't stay here longer than necessary," Alester argued, wincing.
Visenor put a firm hand on the knight's uninjured shoulder. "You protected us. Now it is our turn to protect you. We stay together."
As the third moon drew to a close, the small council convened once more.

The King stood at the head of the table. Jace, Gaelor, the Master of Coin, Maester Cordin, and a few others sat in heavy silence. The King had just laid bare the full extent of the Tyrell and Hightower treason.
Jace looked at his father. "Have the letters from Braavos arrived, Your Grace?"
"They have," the King said darkly. "Derrin Hightower’s transactions with the Tyrells are fully documented. There is no denying it now."

Maester Cordin nervously cleared his throat. "My King, as the Hand has stated, there must be a consequence. But must we confront the Tyrells directly? Humiliating them before the realm could have dire long-term consequences..."

The King stared the maester down until the old man withered. "The Hand has made his thoughts clear, and I agree with him. We will not exaggerate this matter, but we will not diminish it either. My son Gaelor will ride to Highgarden for this very purpose. This isn't just about the Tyrells; the Hightowers are bleeding into this."

The King placed his heavy hands on the table. "This is about justice. And justice is the bedrock of the realm. They did not just plot against my life—had someone else drunk that wine, it would have been my wife or my children. It was a strike against the Seven Kingdoms and House Targaryen. It was a coup."

He looked at Gaelor. "Killing Derrin Hightower was not enough. Justice has not been served. My son Gaelor will serve it. He will demand the head of Derrin’s son from the Hightowers. If they resist, he will take it by force. This is being just."

Jace frowned, leaning forward. "Your Grace, how wise is an 'eye for an eye' approach here? By exposing their plot and imposing crippling sanctions, we punish them severely and turn the situation to our economic advantage."
"We can turn it to our advantage the other way, too," Gaelor interrupted, crossing his arms.
"Our princes are so very right," Maester Cordin chimed in, offering a sycophantic smile. "But perhaps our princes could show a bit of hono—"
Jace raised a single hand, silencing the maester instantly. Cordin bowed his head, clasping his hands and smiling meekly.

"Taking the head of a sixteen-year-old boy, who likely knew absolutely nothing of his father's treason, will not undo the betrayal," Jace argued smoothly. "Even if they cannot retaliate today, it will breed a generational hatred. The Tyrells and Hightowers will harbor a grudge against our children."

"Men I trusted turned their backs on me," the King growled, his eyes burning. "No one turns their back on me and my family. The only reason I am not wiping House Hightower from the map is because Derrin fought by my side years ago."
"I agree with the sentiment, Father, but—" Jace started, then let the words die in his throat, realizing the King's mind was made up.
Gaelor nodded firmly. "I am with the King."

"Then it is decided," the King declared. "The next matter. When the heir returns from Highgarden, we sail for the Stepstones. We will crush the pirates. The Crown Prince will command the campaign. If his brother wishes, he may join him."
Maester Cordin found his opening. "Was this brilliant strategy our Prince's idea?" he asked, practically fawning over Gaelor.
"Who else's would it be?" the King said.
Jace rolled his eyes. "Tell me, Maester Cordin, will there ever be a moment at this table where you offer a thought of your own?"
The maester blinked, offended. "Have I done something to displease you, my prince? I merely speak out of love. I have served this dynasty for years..."
"Derrin used to say the exact same thing," Jace noted dryly.
Jace turned back to the King. "Father, speaking as your notoriously dissenting son, and as your Hand... the impulsiveness of this decision..."
"Impulsive is a heavy word, my prince," the maester interrupted softly.

Jace gave Cordin a look of pure, unadulterated venom, ignoring him entirely. "Can we at least take this step by step? Before we make a final declaration of war in the Stepstones, let us wait for Uncle Baelor to arrive. Let us hear his counsel. Hearing from a man who knows Oldtown’s politics so intimately would be invaluable."
"We are waiting for him regardless," the King agreed. "We cannot put him in danger."
"So," Jace pressed, "when my uncle arrives, do we tell him the decision has already been made, or do we hold a proper council with him?"
Gaelor spoke up, impatient. "Let us see how the Seven Kingdoms react to the Highgarden campaign first, then we march on the Stepstones based on the realm's mood."

The Master of Coin leaned forward, his rings clinking against the wood. "While returning victorious from Highgarden and swelling our coffers sounds wonderful, pouring that new gold directly into a war in the Stepstones—a war with no clear end—would be folly. The gold from Highgarden should be spent on the realm."
"We have the greatest fleet in the world," Gaelor dismissed confidently. "The Stepstones do not frighten me."

Jace looked at the Master of Coin. "While I appreciate your concerns and agree with your financial points... we are not going to Highgarden merely to win a battle or fix our image. We are going there to make sure everyone knows their place. It is a message to the entire realm, not just the Tyrells and Hightowers."
"Of course," the Master of Coin replied delicately, "but marching on House Hightower to execute a sixteen-year-old boy does not mend our image."
"Exactly," Jace said, pointing a finger at him in agreement.
The King slammed his fist on the table, glaring at the Master of Coin. "You deal with the money issues. Leave the rest to us."
The night air was cool against Jace's face as he stood on his balcony, staring out at the sprawling, dark expanse of King's Landing. He took a slow sip of Arbor gold.
From the shadows of his chambers, Weylar Rocke, the Master of Whisperers, crept out. Jace had thrown the man in the black cells once, only to pardon him, ensuring absolute loyalty born of terror.
"How fares my Prince?" Weylar asked, bowing so low he practically scraped the floor, a nervous, sycophantic grin on his face.
Jace didn't turn around. "What is it?"
"My little birds have brought me a song, my Prince."

Weylar bent his neck further, rubbing his hands together. "My Prince, I do not wish to overstep... but it seems our beloved Prince Gaelor was seen leaving a rather low brothel in the city. Just nights after his own wedding."

Jace stopped. The balcony was utterly silent, save for the distant crashing of waves against the cliffs of the Red Keep. Weylar’s smile faltered, replaced by a creeping dread as Jace remained entirely still. Had he miscalculated? Would Jace take his brother's side and have Weylar's tongue out?
Slowly, Jace turned. The moonlight caught the faint, dangerous smirk on his lips.
He walked over to the silver flagon on the table, poured a second goblet of the rich vintage, and held it out to the spymaster.

title: Chapter V: Fathers and Their Sins
synopsis: As Prince Gaelor rides to deliver the King's ruthless retribution, a devastating confrontation forever scars the realm. Meanwhile, perilous secrets take root in the shadows, and a broken knight desperately seeks sanctuary.
content: Morning light crept through the heavy velvet curtains of the royal apartments. Maela lay awake, quietly watching Jace as he slept, his silver hair spilling across the pillows. The quiet of the dawn was abruptly shattered by the earth-rattling roar of a dragon.
Saera had returned from the North. She had meant to be gone for three or four days, but a full week had passed.

Jace, Maela, and Gaelor went to the courtyards to meet her. As Saera dismounted her massive beast, the freezing winds of the North still seemed to cling to her cloak. Almost immediately, her violet eyes dropped to the golden Hand’s pin fastened to Jace’s chest.
“Welcome back,” Jace said, his voice smooth and guarded.
“The North is beautiful, Jace. You truly must see it,” Saera said, pulling off her heavy leather gloves.
Jace offered a thin, aristocratic smile. “You know how I loathe the cold.”
Saera stepped past him and embraced Maela warmly. As they turned to walk inside the keep, Saera lowered her voice. “Is Father furious?”
“He has not seen you in days,” Jace replied plainly. “If I were him, I would be.”
They walked through the shadowed corridors of the Red Keep. “What is the mood here?” Saera asked, sensing the thick tension in the air.
Jace sighed. “Father and Gaelor are terrified we might actually have a moment without a war, so they are looking to start new ones. We are waiting for Great-uncle Baelor to arrive.”
“Baelor is coming?” Saera asked, her brows rising in surprise.
“Finally, something positive might happen,” Jace muttered.

“Since our Princess has returned,” a reedy voice echoed from the balcony above. Maester Cordin stood there, his chain of office clinking against the stone rail. “The King will wish to speak with her. Furthermore, His Grace has commanded that the council convene tonight for one final time.”
Jace stopped, his eyes narrowing at the old man. “But Baelor has not arrived yet.”
“I know only what I am told, my Prince,” the Maester replied with an irritatingly polite bow. “It is the King's command.”
Jace took a step forward, his voice turning dangerously soft. “What exactly do you know, Maester?”
Cordin offered only silence and a blank stare.
Jace turned back to Saera, his jaw tight. “You heard him.” Together, they walked inside.
The air in Queen Jaery's chambers was thick with the scent of lavender and myrrh. The Queen sat near the hearth with Saera, Maela, and little Vhaemys playing on the Myrish rug.
“Where have you been all these days?” Jaery scolded Saera, though her voice held more exhaustion than true anger. “Your father is furious.”
“The North was too beautiful to leave in haste,” Saera replied smoothly. “I assume Jace will speak to Father on my behalf, now that he is the Hand.”
Jaery sighed, waving a dismissive hand. “We discuss your futures every day,” she said, looking pointedly between Saera and Maela. The implication of marriage hung heavy in the room.
The Queen turned to Maela. “In fact, there are a few suitors I would like you to meet. It might be good for you to take a journey. Leave the capital for a time.”
Maela, gently stroking the small, scaled head of the hatchling dragon—Visenor’s former beast—coiled around her arm, nodded slowly. “Who?”
“Lord Arryn could be a fine match,” Jaery suggested.
Maela wrinkled her nose. “He is far too old. I want someone fresh... someone with life in him.”

Jaery hesitated. “There is another. Though how we would explain it to the Lord Hand or your father, I do not know. Their introduction in the throne room was... less than ideal.” She was speaking of Steffon Baratheon, the Young Stag. King Baelenys loved Lord Baratheon and was entirely unaware of the young lord's insolence toward Jace.
“How would you feel about him?” Jaery asked cautiously.
Maela smirked, her Targaryen blood warming to the challenge. “I could meet with him. I could meet with Lord Arryn as well. I have no objections.”
Jaery looked relieved, glad that the girl was willing to leave the stifling air of the keep. “It will depend heavily on your father's mood, of course.”
“Are you not going to tell us what is happening?” Saera pressed, her eyes darting between her mother and sister.
Jaery’s face hardened. “For now, the fewer people who know, the better.”

The air in the capital had grown heavy and stagnant, the frantic energy of the immediate aftermath settling into a grim, bureaucratic routine as the map of Westeros was spread across the polished table of the small council chambers, illuminated by thick beeswax candles.

The King stood at the head of the table. Jace, Gaelor, the Master of Coin, Maester Cordin, and a few others sat in heavy silence. The King had just laid bare the full extent of the Tyrell and Hightower treason.
“Now that we are agreed on all fronts,” King Baelenys rumbled, leaning over the painted table, “we will announce the treason of Derrin Hightower and the Tyrells to the realm before nightfall.”
“A wise decision, Your Grace,” Maester Cordin agreed immediately. “The moment it is announced, we must move. Strike before they can muster a response.”
Jace interjected, keeping his voice level. “Your Grace, your uncle, Maester Baelor, has not yet arrived, though he must be close. Should we not wait for his counsel?”

“I have decided this is not a matter that requires my uncle's waiting,” the King said, his tone leaving no room for argument. “There is no sense in delaying plans that are already set in stone. Besides, my uncle is the 'Jace' of his generation.” The King offered his Hand a faint, knowing smirk. Baelor had been effectively exiled to Oldtown years ago for being too much of a scholar and too argumentative—exactly like the son standing before him. “And you know perfectly well who the least desired person at a war council is.”
The King turned his massive shoulders toward his heir. “Gaelor, you will ride out with a vanguard immediately.”
Gaelor stood taller. “You want this boy's head, Father?”
“You want it too,” the King said darkly. “They came for our blood.”
Gaelor nodded sharply and left the chambers to prepare for war.

Jace watched his brother leave, a knot forming in his stomach. “Very well, Your Grace. But I highly doubt a fifteen-year-old boy was made privy to a grand treasonous plot. Do we truly need to take his head? Must the son pay for the sins of the father?”

Maester Cordin leaned in, and for the first time, spoke with cold, undeniable logic. “My Prince, high lords answer for the lives of their children. And sometimes... sons must pay the price for their fathers' ambitions.”
Jace stared at the Maester, finding the brutal truth in the old man's words. He pressed his lips together and remained silent.
“If there is nothing else,” the King commanded, “make the preparations.”
Later, in the quiet of the royal corridors, Maela found Jace standing by a high window.
“Mother wishes me to meet with Lord Arryn and Lord Steffon Baratheon,” Maela told him, watching his reaction closely. “What do you think?”
Jace let out a breath of pure disdain. “Lord Arryn is near forty. Is he not a bit... ancient for you?”
