Jace turned to her, his brow furrowed. “Why would you even accept such an offer? By all means, entertain yourself if you wish, but do you truly need to stoop to this? Why demean yourself?”
“Mother says it is time I married,” Maela reasoned playfully. “And an alliance with House Baratheon would be a strong diplomatic tie, would it not?”
“What need have we for it?” Jace scoffed, his voice dripping with Targaryen arrogance. “Stags are merely meals for dragons, Maela. Do not force yourself into anything you do not desire.”

Weeks of travel had worn the leather of their saddles and the soles of their boots to nothing by the time the Torrentine’s rushing waters finally broke the horizon. Rhaella slumped against Visenor’s back, her arms wrapped weakly around his waist as their exhausted horse plodded forward.

They had finally reached the rushing waters. As soon as they stopped, Visenor helped Rhaella down so she could drink from the icy stream. Alester dismounted with a heavy grunt. He was walking with a severe limp, his face still a canvas of black bruises and dried blood, though he moved with grim determination.
“I will settle you in a village nearby,” Alester rasped, adjusting his sword belt. “Then I must take to the road again.”
Visenor looked at the broken knight in disbelief. “You intend to ride back? In your condition?”
“I am already well past the deadline I promised Prince Jace,” Alester said stubbornly. “I should leave at once.”
“Prince Jace is not a fool. He will have anticipated delays,” Visenor argued.
“No one could anticipate what I survived in those woods, my prince,” Alester replied softly.
They rode further, drawing closer to a small, quiet village nestled among the trees. Visenor, however, felt a prickle at the back of his neck. The hairs on his arms stood up. He felt eyes on them.
“Stop,” Visenor whispered to Alester, scanning the dense foliage.

Suddenly, a rustle in the underbrush caught his eye. Visenor threw himself off his horse and charged. A scrawny, rat-faced man burst from the bushes, sprinting for his life. Visenor tackled him into the dirt, drawing his dagger in a flash and pressing the cold steel to the man's throat.
Alester sighed internally, a deep, tired sound. “Prince, stay your hand,” the knight called out, painfully dismounting and limping over to the scuffle.
Alester hauled the scrawny man to his feet by his collar. The man brushed the dirt from his tunic, entirely unfazed by the blade that had just been at his neck.
“Ser Dayne,” the man said with a sly grin. “Had you killed me, you would have had a very difficult time explaining it to Lady Dayne.”

Alester’s one good eye closed for a moment. It was Lorenah Dayne’s Master of Whisperers. To have survived sellswords and assassins only to be caught by a spy practically on their doorstep was a bitter pill.
“What do we do?” Alester muttered, turning to Visenor apologetically. “I pray Lady Dayne will consider my friendship with Prince Jace and not view this as an intrusion.”

The whisperer looked at Visenor and Rhaella, clicking his tongue. “They say it is true, Ser Dayne. You surround yourself only with silver hair and purple eyes.” The man rubbed his fingers together. “Give me coin, and I will take you directly to Lady Dayne at Starfall... and then I will forget I ever saw you.”
Alester grabbed the man's tunic again. “You would betray your own lady? Are you threatening us?”

“Lady Dayne has eyes everywhere, Ser,” the spy replied smoothly. “My lips are sealed, but if someone else saw you in these woods... well, their lips might not be. A little coin ensures my complete dedication to your stealth.”
Grumbling, Alester tossed the man the last of his silver.

Hours later, the towering, beautiful pale stone of Starfall rose before them. Lady Lorenah Dayne was standing on a balcony overlooking the rushing waters, watching the sunrise with her bare feet dipping into a shallow pool.
She turned as they were led in. “Ser Dayne?” she said, confused, before her eyes landed on Visenor and Rhaella. The color drained from her face.
She stood up immediately. “My Prince. This is... not the most pleasant of surprises,” she said, managing a strained smile. “I only wish you had arrived under more strategically sound circumstances.”
“I wish the same, my lady,” Visenor said solemnly.
Alester stepped forward, bowing stiffly. “My lady, we come to your halls on behalf of Prince Jacaelon. He placed his trust in you from the very first day. We ask for your sanctuary.”

Lorenah listened as Alester briefly explained the treason and their flight. She let out a long breath, pinching the bridge of her nose. “You may stay. No one will dare poke their nose into Starfall, not a Martell, not anyone. But you must understand... at some point, you will have to leave.”
Visenor bowed his head respectfully. “When the time is right, I hope to repay your boundless hospitality.”
Lorenah offered a sad, knowing smile. “Let us hope you are still in a position to do so, my Prince.”
Refusing to rest, Alester immediately turned to Lorenah. “My lady, I must request a fresh horse. I need to depart for King's Landing at once.”
Night had fallen over the Kingsroad. Gaelor’s vanguard moved like a great, iron serpent. Two thousand men marched behind the Targaryen banners.
At the same time, a raven took flight from the Red Keep, carrying Jace’s decrees of crippling taxes and sanctions to Oldtown.

The convoy ground to a halt. The only sound in the dead of night was the splashing of urine against stone as Prince Gaelor relieved himself against a large oak tree. Two thousand heavily armed men waited in absolute silence for the heir to finish.
Saathos rode up beside him, grinning in the dark. “Need me to hold it for you, my Prince?”
Gaelor hitched his breeches and shot him a dark look. “Make one more out-of-place joke, Saathos, and I'll cut off your little Saathos and hand it to you.”
Four days later, the massive, towering silhouette of Oldtown loomed in the night.
The Knights of Oldtown rode out to meet them, bearing torches and speaking with the utmost courtesy. “We are honored by your presence, my Prince,” their captain said. “Please, enter.”

Gaelor sat tall in his saddle, his face like carved stone. “I am not here on a holiday. I am here to resolve a matter of treason. If you resist, you bring the wrath of the Seven Kingdoms upon yourselves.”
The knights exchanged confused glances. “My Prince, we received the King's raven regarding the taxes. Is the matter not already settled?”
Gaelor ignored the question. “We come by the King's command. Do not make this difficult.”

At the gates of the Hightower, they were blocked. A young, handsome knight in his early twenties, standing with perfect posture, stepped forward. Ser Brant Costayne drew his sword with visible reluctance.

“My Prince,” Costayne said, his voice echoing in the courtyard. “We took an oath. In the name of House Hightower, and trusting in your noble grace... you cannot enter.” Behind him, the other knights shifted uneasily, hands resting on their pommels.
Through the high windows of the keep, Gaelor saw the silhouettes of two young girls peering down. The moment he looked up, they vanished into the depths of the castle.
“You are an honorable knight, Ser Costayne,” Gaelor said, his voice booming. “But the house you protect has committed treason against the Crown.”

“With all due respect, my Prince,” Costayne replied tightly, “that was the work of the dishonorable Derrin Hightower, not his kin.” Oldtown boasted a standing army of three thousand men; they were not easily intimidated.
“Any blood spilled here tonight will have consequences far beyond these walls,” Gaelor warned.

A figure appeared at the window. Lord Timos Hightower, a boy of no more than fifteen or sixteen. “Ser Costayne,” the boy's voice cracked slightly. “Let us not escalate this. Let the Prince enter with a few of his men so we may speak.”
Saathos leaned close to Gaelor. “I'll have our boys fan out. Surround the perimeter, just in case they try to rally.”
“Prepare for the worst,” Gaelor muttered back.
As Saathos began aggressively detaining maesters, servants, and anyone in the courtyard, Gaelor marched into the Great Hall with his personal guard.
Lord Timos knelt before the Iron Throne's heir. The boy was barely five and a half feet tall, his legs visibly trembling beneath his rich velvet doublet.
“My Lord,” Gaelor announced coldly. “Your house is guilty of high treason. You conspired to murder the King and the royal family. This is proven.”
Timos looked up, tears welling in his eyes. “But... but the raven this morning! Did my House and the Tyrells not settle this with the Crown? We bent the knee!”

“A strike against the King does not go unpunished,” Gaelor stated. He looked down at the shaking boy. “Do not throw away your life and risk your entire house. You are already in disgrace. Give your life here, now, and save your name from total ruin.”
Timos panicked. With a desperate cry, the boy drew his sword.
From the corners of the hall, his two young sisters began to scream. Gaelor’s men surged forward, tackling the girls to the stone floor and pinning them down.
Timos charged blindly at the Prince.
Gaelor drew his blade in a flash of steel, parrying the clumsy strike and knocking the boy to the ground with a heavy blow. Timos scrambled up, sobbing, and rushed him again.
At that moment, the heavy oak doors of the hall were forced open. Ser Costayne and a dozen knights rushed in.

Gaelor didn't hesitate. He swung his longsword in a brutal, flawless arc. The steel bit through flesh and bone. Timos Hightower's decapitated head hit the floor with a sickening thud, rolling to a stop perfectly at Gaelor’s boots.
Costayne froze, his eyes wide with horror as he stared at the headless body of his young lord. His sword trembled in his grip as the Oldtown knights moved to encircle the Targaryens.

“Consider this the King's mercy!” Gaelor roared over the screaming of the girls. “We could have wiped your entire house from the maps of Westeros! Stand down and obey your new orders, or the next time we return, it will be with dragons!”
Costayne looked at the hysterical girls pinned to the floor. He stood paralyzed.

One of Gaelor's captains tapped the prince's shoulder and pointed to the door. Slowly, keeping their swords drawn, Gaelor and his men backed out of the hall. Every Oldtown guard they passed reached for their hilts, but the sheer shock of the butchery kept their blades sheathed.
Gaelor emerged into the cool night air of the courtyard. Seconds later, a figure appeared in the high window above.
“Knights of Oldtown!” Ser Costayne screamed, his voice breaking with grief and rage. “Your Lord was murdered under a banner of peace! Kill Gaelor! Kill them all!”
Hell broke loose. Two hundred Oldtown knights drew steel against Gaelor's massive vanguard. The courtyard turned into a slaughterhouse.
Inside the keep, in the blind, blood-mad panic of the ensuing chaos, one of the soldiers drove a blade through one of the little Hightower girls.

The battle in the courtyard was swift and merciless. The Oldtown knights, vastly outnumbered, were slaughtered to the last man. Ser Costayne died drowning in his own blood on the cobblestones. It was a massacre that the Citadel and the Reach would remember for a hundred years.
Covered in the blood of the Reach, Prince Gaelor and his army left Oldtown behind them.
The sea winds battered the impossibly thick walls of Storm's End. Princess Maela arrived to find the Young Stag himself waiting to greet her.

“It is an immense honor to welcome Princess Maela to the Stormlands,” Steffon Baratheon said, bowing low. “Especially after the... misunderstandings with your twin brother in the capital. Your presence honors us.”
Maela offered a polite, regal nod.
“I can show you the castle, if you wish,” Steffon offered eagerly.

Steffon snapped his fingers, sending servants scrambling to prepare a feast. Later, he took her to a beautiful, wind-swept cliffside near the ancient statues of the Baratheon ancestors. Her Kingsguard and maids stood a discreet distance away.
They sat on the grass. Steffon was surprisingly earnest, talking at length about the history of his home.
“My Princess,” Steffon asked carefully, plucking a blade of grass. “Did you come here of your own accord?”
Maela raised an eyebrow. “Of course. No one forces me to do anything I do not wish to do.”
“Forgive me, but you are rarely seen outside the Red Keep. I only thought... perhaps you were commanded to come.”
“I simply prefer the capital,” she said. They spoke of her desire to leave the suffocating politics of the court for a while.
Steffon looked at her curiously. “You do not ride a dragon, do you? I have heard there are many unclaimed beasts residing on Dragonstone and Driftmark.”
“I do have a dragon,” Maela corrected him, thinking of the small hatchling now in her care.
“Ah,” Steffon said, completely oblivious to his own arrogance. “Well, to repay your visit, I had thought I could go to Dragonstone and fetch a dragon for you.”
Maela burst into laughter at the sheer, ridiculous audacity of the statement. Steffon looked genuinely confused, not understanding why she was laughing.

She asked him about war and battles. Steffon admitted he rarely fought unless a true war was called; he preferred the glory of the tourney grounds. They spent the entire day together. To Maela's surprise, the young lord was a perfect gentleman. He made no crass advances, treating her with the awkward, respectful chivalry of a seventeen-year-old boy who spent more time with swords than women. Maela, amused, offered only distant, flirtatious smiles in return.
That night, in her chambers, Maela called for her sworn shield, Ser Vance. It was the first time she had truly paid attention to the man in the white cloak.
She poured him a cup of wine. “What is your opinion of the Young Stag, Ser Vance?”
Vance stood stiffly. “It is not my place to have an opinion, Princess.”
“We have known each other a long time. Your thoughts matter to me.”
Vance hesitated. “I believe... I share the thoughts of your twin brother, Princess. A stag is no fit match for a dragon.”

“Lord Arryn is an honorable man,” Vance said carefully. “But it is said he has fallen into a deep melancholy since his wife passed. A young, vibrant princess such as yourself might find little joy at his side.”
Maela stepped closer, looking up into his eyes. “What of your future, Ser Vance? Do you have plans?”
“I swore an oath. I am a servant of the Crown.”
“Do you have regrets? Have you ever known a woman?”
Vance’s breathing grew shallow. “I am bound by my duty. I am bound only to serve your desires.” He swallowed hard. “What is it you want me to say, Princess?”
“I don't know,” Maela whispered. The wine had made her bold. She reached up and kissed him.
Vance froze, then panicked as her hands went to the straps of his heavy white armor. “Princess... we are in Storm's End. What if someone comes?”
“Hush,” she whispered, pulling him closer, easily soothing his fears.
That night, Princess Maela surrendered her maidenhead to her Kingsguard.
The next morning, before they took to the road, Maela quietly instructed her most trusted maid to brew her moon tea. She drank it in secret, ensuring no Baratheon servant would ever be the wiser.
Steffon saw them off at the gates, loudly proclaiming his joy at her visit.
On the road back, Ser Vance rode far behind her carriage, silent and brooding. The reality of his broken vows was crushing him.
When they stopped to rest the horses, Maela sought him out. “Are you going to behave like this the entire journey?” she teased gently.
“Princess,” Vance hissed, his eyes wide with fear. “I have broken my sacred oath. I have done a terrible thing. It is a heavy burden.”
Maela frowned slightly. “What do you mean? Did you feel nothing with me?”
“How could I not?” Vance said, his voice thick with emotion. “Of course I felt it.”
“Then there is no need to feel this way,” she said, touching his arm.
Vance pulled back slightly, terrified. “But I have put my life in grave danger.”
Maela offered a soft, reassuring smile. “Do not fear, Ser Vance. What happened between us stays strictly between us.”
The dust of the Kingsroad rose in great clouds as two massive convoys met just outside the gates of King's Landing.
Prince Gaelor, returning from the massacre at Oldtown, found his path blocked by the banners of the Citadel. Great-uncle Baelor Targaryen rode at the head of the arriving column.
Baelor, an older man with sharp eyes and a neatly trimmed scholar's beard, laughed as he rode forward. “Gaelor and Baelor, eh?” He bowed his head respectfully. “My Prince.”
“You have missed much,” Gaelor said grimly. They spoke of the Tyrell treason and the bloody business in the Reach.
Baelor’s face fell as he listened. “You killed young Timos Hightower?”
“By the King's command?” Baelor asked, his voice tightening.
Baelor stared at his great-nephew, the political ramifications crashing down upon him in an instant. The King had moved without him, and he had moved brutally.
Panic flared in the old scholar's eyes. “Where is Prince Jacaelon?”
“In the Red Keep, I assume,” Gaelor replied, confused by the man's sudden dread.
Without another word, Baelor spurred his horse, galloping frantically toward the towering red walls of the capital.

title: Chapter VI: A Crown of Thorns
synopsis: As King Baelenys descends into violent paranoia, a devastating confrontation shatters the royal family from within. While the Red Keep suffocates in the aftermath, Visenor uncovers ancient Targaryen secrets and an unexpected glimmer of hope far away in Dorne.
content: Jace’s muscles burned with the familiar ache of exertion. In Ser Alester’s absence, he spent his free hours in the training yard crossing steel with Ser Orwell Morrigen. For years, Jace had practically served as Alester’s own squire—spared the menial chores of fetching wine or polishing armor, but subjected to every grueling lesson of the sword—and under the knight's tutelage, the Prince had grown into a remarkably capable blade.

When Jace wasn't training, his mind darkened with thoughts of little Vhaemys. The girl’s uncanny resemblance to their father, King Baelenys, unsettled him deeply. Jace feared the child was being molded into a psychopath. He had already tasked Weylar Rocke with quietly scouring the city for a tutor—someone who understood the minds of children, someone who could teach the girl empathy and careful thought. Vhaemys spent too much time with the King, idolizing the man's cruelty and mimicking his every volatile gesture.

To clear his head, Jace sought out the Dragonpit. He spent his remaining hours with Jhagar, his dragon, speaking to the beast in low High Valyrian, teaching it commands and forging a bond that felt far more reliable than the blood of his own family.
The courtyard of Starfall was bathed in the pale light of dawn as Lady Lorenah Dayne’s convoy prepared to depart. Ser Alester stood before Visenor, leaning heavily on a walking stick.
They embraced, the knight wincing slightly. "My prince," Alester rasped, his voice thick with emotion. "We may never meet again. I pray you absolve me of any debts, and that I have served you well."
"You have, Ser Alester. More than I could ever ask," Visenor said softly.

Before mounting her palfrey, Lady Dayne provided Visenor and Rhaella with instructions for their sanctuary—an old, secluded retreat once used by Dornish scholars. It was remote, disorganized, but overflowing with ancient texts. She had already dispatched a maester and a maid to attend to them.
"Do not worry about the whisperer you encountered in the woods," Lorenah told Visenor with a sharp smile. "Alester told me everything. I will handle him."
Once the convoy departed, Visenor and Rhaella were escorted to the scholar’s retreat. The promised maid and maester were already there, setting the dusty chambers to rights.

Rhaella stood in the center of the cluttered room, wrapping her shawl tighter. "What do we do now?" she asked, her voice trembling slightly. "We aren't going to your father. Fine. We escaped all of that... but are we to spend the rest of our lives running? There is a throne waiting for you."
Visenor stepped closer, taking her hands. "I am perfectly content to disappear and never return," Rhaella added, "but what is it you want?"

"We cannot stay here forever," Visenor admitted, his violet eyes scanning the towering stacks of parchment. "Lady Dayne is helping us, but she cannot protect us eternally. For now, we will use this silence to our advantage. We will rest. Later... later I will return to take what is mine. The realm may fall into chaos, and perhaps we can use that to our benefit. Perhaps, in the meantime, we will even have a child." He smiled warmly. "For now, I am going to look at these books."
As the maester excused himself to gather provisions, the maid approached Rhaella to tend to her exhaustion.
The Dornish maester returned hours later, dropping a sack of supplies onto a wooden table.

"My prince, I bring news from the realm, though you might have expected it," the maester said, his Dornish drawl heavy and his tongue surprisingly foul. "The Hand of the King is dead. The man who plotted against your father was Derrin Hightower, working in bed with the Tyrells. The Crown has leveled crippling embargoes against Highgarden. The Tyrells are utterly fucked."

The maester poured himself a cup of water and continued. "Furthermore, Prince Gaelor arrived in Oldtown this morning. Even after the Tyrells had supposedly reached an agreement with the Crown, the Prince descended on the city with two thousand men. They butchered Lord Timos and Alysanne Hightower."

"Aye. Two hundred knights of Oldtown were slaughtered alongside them," the maester said grimly. "They say when the massacre was done, the blood squelched inside Prince Gaelor's armor with every step he took. There are rumors his men kicked Lord Timos’s head around the courtyard like a bladder. Half the realm now fully expects Prince Gaelor to be named heir."
"Of course they do," Visenor said bitterly, his gaze returning to the parchment. "He is as cruel as my father." He found himself liking the blunt, unpolished maester.

"Anyway," the maester said, gesturing to the chaotic piles of books. "As for all these scrolls... I am no great scholar, but I imagine it is a paradise for a man like you. Many maesters from Oldtown and Dorne resided here over the years. They wrote constantly. You will find lifetimes of correspondence. You might even find letters written by your great-uncle, Baelor Targaryen."
"Why are these kept here and not at the Citadel?" Visenor asked.
"Because they are unfinished," the maester shrugged. "The authors abandoned them or passed on, and the pages simply accumulated."
"And will we be safe from foreign eyes here?"
"Absolutely," the maester assured him. "We will say the retreat is full. No one will come."

For five hours, Visenor buried himself in the dust of history, desperate to escape the bloody reality of his family. He sifted through random correspondences. One crumpled letter, discarded a year prior, caught his eye. It was an exchange between two maesters discussing the southward migration of dragons, heavily referencing Baelor Targaryen's work.
The author noted: "I have not found much, but I believe Boneskin is currently roosting near the Red Mountains. The beast is quiet, and the middle peaks suit it well."

Boneskin. Visenor knew the name. A dragon small and fragile in appearance, with wings so thin the bones showed through the membrane. It wasn't malnourished; it simply ate little by choice. It avoided humans, not out of aggression, but because it was believed to possess an intelligence far surpassing a normal dragon. The maester had considered writing to Baelor about it but feared being dismissed. Boneskin loved the mountains because it desired isolation, not because it was a threat to the flocks.
Visenor found himself utterly fascinated by the conscious nature of the beast. He wanted to investigate further, entertaining the thought of an excursion into the Red Mountains to verify the claim.
Hours later, he climbed the stone stairs to the upper chambers. The maid met him at the door, beaming.
"My prince," she whispered. "Our Princess... she is with child."
Visenor froze, his breath catching in his throat. Joy surged through him. He rushed inside to find Rhaella weeping tears of pure happiness.

Before leaving the room, Visenor pulled the maid aside. "You are not to speak a word of this to Lady Dayne," he instructed strictly. "If she knows, it could spread, and it would put her in terrible danger for harboring us."
He returned to Rhaella, brushing a tear from her cheek. "We are safe for now, my love, but in a month or two, we will have to live even further from the world's eyes."
"Do you think I could take a walk in the gardens out back?" she asked softly.
Visenor nodded. They walked together beneath the shade of the olive trees. There was no one else for miles. The only sound was the howling of the wind through the canyon.
A sudden thought pierced Visenor's mind: I am supposed to want my throne, but for the first time in my life, I am at peace.
He looked at Rhaella, the wind pulling at her silver hair. "Let's just never go back," he joked lightly. "It's rather nice here."
Rhaella laughed, the sound carrying away into the Dornish mountains.
Before Gaelor or Baelor could return to the capital, the dark news reached Jace.
He was walking across the courtyard when Queen Jaery stormed past him, a blur of silk and fury. Jace called out, but he couldn't catch her as she marched toward the inner keep.
Weylar Rocke materialized from the shadows a moment later. "My Prince," the spymaster murmured. "Prince Gaelor struck Oldtown with two thousand men. They took Lord Timos's head."
Princess Saera, who had just walked up beside Jace, scoffed. "And?"
Weylar swallowed hard. "Princess Alysanne was also murdered. Hung from a window. Rumor has it the men played games with Lord Timos's severed head."
Jace dragged a hand down his face. "Fucking stupid," he hissed.
Saera’s eyes blazed with disgust. "He is a deranged psychopath."
Together, they marched to the King's solar. King Baelenys sat at his table, staring broodingly into the hearth. The unexpected death of the girl had complicated his brutal message.

"Father, what is the meaning of this?" Saera demanded the moment the doors shut behind Jace's dismissed guards. "Was it not enough to murder a little boy? You had the girl butchered too? Will you have me killed next to make things just? Must I die too?"
Jace stepped forward. "The King ordered no such thing. The girl was not his doing."
Saera wheeled on the King, her voice dripping with venom. "Oh, so your other spineless son disobeyed you to commit murder? The son you are trying to make heir has gone rabid?"
The King’s face turned the color of bruised plum. He stood, his massive frame casting a terrifying shadow. "I will throw you in the black cells, girl!"
Jace put himself between them. "Take this wench away from me!" the King roared.
Jace grabbed his sister's arm. "Go to my chambers. We will speak there."
Saera ripped her arm away, sneering at Jace's chest. "Does the Hand's pin make you a man now?"
"Know your place, Saera!" Jace snapped. "Does screaming at us ease your conscience? Go to your room!"
Saera stormed out, cursing under her breath.
The King sank heavily back into his chair. "Stupidity. Utter nonsense."
"If only someone had warned you," Jace said coldly.
"Gaelor did not do this," the King muttered defensively.
"Gaelor and every man who rode with him are responsible," Jace shot back. "And so are you. How could you trust that man?"
The King reached for his flagon of wine, but Jace snatched it away. The King glared at him, a muscle ticking in his jaw. "In the end... it is just a girl."
Jace leaned over the table, his eyes burning with quiet fury. "Tell me you did not mean that. Take it back, or you will regret it."
The King scoffed. "Does this offend you so deeply, Jacaelon Hightower?"
Jace froze. "Are you truly calling me a Hightower?"
"We will speak when Gaelor returns," the King dismissed.
"I will not share a room with that butcher," Jace spat. "If Saera or Maela meet the same fate tomorrow, will you say it is just a girl?"
"We are the only ones with the luxury to do so," the King growled.
"This is not being just," Jace said, his voice laced with disgust.
"You know nothing of justice," the King roared. "I hope your brother teaches it to you!"

"We murdered a lord!" Jace yelled back. "We murdered an innocent girl! We slaughtered two hundred knights! The sadistic imbecile you thrust into the council couldn't solve a simple conflict without a massacre!"
The doors creaked open, and Maester Cordin stepped in hesitantly.
"Take him away," the King ordered the Maester, waving a dismissive hand at his Hand.
Cordin bowed nervously. "My Prince, please, let us take a walk."

Jace glared at his father one last time. "If we ever listen to that idiot again, if we send him out to hang and butcher men on a whim, we will pay a price we cannot afford. It will not happen all at once, but step by step, we will turn the entire realm against us."
Jace turned on his heel and left. He went straight to Saera's chambers. She was furiously packing her riding leathers, preparing to fly away on her dragon.
"Saera," Jace said, closing the door. "I have stood by you in this castle at every turn. I am always here."
"Were you not at that council?" she spat, tossing a tunic into a trunk.
"Did I ask to hang and butcher them?"
Saera grabbed him by the collar, yanking roughly on the Hand's pin. "If you cannot change a fucking thing, what good are you?"

Jace gently but firmly removed her hands. "I have been Hand for two weeks. What did you expect me to change? I stood behind every word I said, and I voiced them openly. Father already compares me to Uncle Baelor. What more can I do? I tell him to be pragmatic. Provoking the King accomplishes absolutely nothing."

The door swung open, and little Vhaemys trotted in, a vicious grin on her face. "Good for them!" the girl chimed in childishly. "The dragon crushed Oldtown! My brother made the people who tried to hurt us pay!"
Jace pointed at the door. "Go to your room, Vhaemys."
The girl stuck her tongue out at the Hand of the King, turned, and ran down the hall to find Maela.
Jace looked at Saera. "I need to fly," she muttered.
"I'll come with you," Jace said. They mounted their dragons and took to the skies, flying toward the serene lake in the hills, far from the stench of the Red Keep.
The day Baelor and Gaelor arrived, the Red Keep was a powder keg.

Baelor stormed into the Great Hall, ignoring the lords and ladies who bowed and fawned over him. Gaelor walked behind him, his face a mask of sullen anger; he was entirely displeased with how the princess's death had tainted his victory.
For days, the Queen had not left her chambers. She refused to share the King's bed.
Maester Cordin paled when he saw Baelor, knowing his position as Grand Maester was likely forfeit. "Maester Baelor, welcome—"
"We must convene the council immediately," Baelor interrupted, cutting Cordin off entirely. "Tonight, if His Grace is available."
Word had already reached the capital that the Tyrells were seething. The massacre had bred a deep, burning hatred for the Targaryens in the Reach. The King agreed to the council.
As the lords gathered outside the chamber doors, Jace saw Baelor and embraced him tightly. "Welcome home."
"I am not welcome, nor am I glad to be here," Baelor said grimly.
"I know," Jace whispered. "But it is good to see you."
They turned to enter, but Jace signaled his Kingsguard. "Prince Gaelor is barred from this chamber."
The guards hesitated, shifting uncomfortably. Gaelor glared at his brother. "I need to be in there, Jace."
"Your presence is not required," Jace said flatly.
"You have no idea what happened out there!" Gaelor argued, stepping forward.
"We heard exactly what happened out there," Jace replied.
The guards crossed their halberds, blocking the heir's path. "By order of the Hand," the captain muttered apologetically.
Gaelor shoved the guards aside, but Jace stepped directly into his path, chest to chest. "Get out."
"You don't get to decide that," Gaelor hissed. "I am not pleased with what happened either."
Jace calmly brought a hand to his chest, casually blowing a speck of dust off his golden Hand’s pin. "Your opinion is irrelevant. You are not coming in."
Gaelor’s eyes narrowed. "Shouldn't you be happy that things went sour for me?"
Jace looked at him as if he were looking at a rabid dog. "Are you a halfwit? Do you truly believe I would take joy in the slaughter of two innocent children and two hundred men?"
"Why are you not inside?" the King's booming voice echoed down the hall as he approached.
"Jace won't let me in, Father," Gaelor complained.
The King scowled. "Take your seats. Both of you."
Jace held his ground. "Gaelor may give his report, and then he will leave."
The King flared with anger, but Baelor spoke up before a shouting match could begin. "Please, Your Grace. Let us just convene."
They sat at the heavy table, placing their painted markers on the map.
Jace looked across the table at his brother. "Where are your markers, boy?"
Gaelor sneered. "My stones are right here. Want me to give them to you?"
The room quieted. The King looked to his uncle. "From this day forth, Maester Baelor shall serve as our Grand Maester."
Cordin bowed his head, maintaining a tight, sycophantic smile, betraying no insult.
"Uncle," the King continued, his voice softening marginally. "I did not wish to welcome you back under such circumstances, but this is where we stand."

"How could this happen?" Baelor demanded, his voice trembling with righteous fury. "An order to execute a prince and a little girl? This command befits neither my nephew nor my King! We have lost an entire Great House. We have permanently alienated a house that should have been our loyal subjects!"
The King looked at Gaelor. "Let the Prince explain."
Gaelor recounted the events, insisting that the Oldtown knights had drawn first, that the massacre was an unavoidable necessity born of their defiance.
Baelor slammed a hand on the table. "The decision was flawed from the beginning! Why send two thousand men to execute a fifteen-year-old boy? Was this slaughter not the inevitable outcome?"

"What's done is done," the King declared coldly. "And we shall offer no apologies for it. The Hightowers brought this upon themselves. They have learned never to cross the Targaryens again. Unfortunately, there were unintended casualties. There is no sense in dwelling on it."
Baelor stared at the King in disbelief. "Then how do we proceed from here? My mind cannot grasp this madness."
"Grand Maester Baelor, do not interrupt constantly," the King warned.
"Then why did you summon me here?" Baelor challenged.
"I brought you here for your own safety, and because you are my blood, I made you Grand Maester."
"Then why convene this council at all?" Maester Cordin threw his hands up. "Make your decrees, send your ravens! Why gather us if our counsel means nothing?"
"Shut your mouth!" both the King and Jace snapped simultaneously. They exchanged a brief, surprised look.

Jace leaned back, gesturing to the room. "But Uncle, if we don't gather, how will these sycophants pretend to advise the King while feeding off the Crown's scraps?" Jace looked pointedly at Gaelor and Cordin.
"It was the King's command," Gaelor said through gritted teeth. "I executed it."
"I take full responsibility," the King stated loudly. "I know Gaelor did not order the girl's death."
Lord Tion Lannister, the Master of Laws, cleared his throat. "A new page has been written for Prince Gaelor in the annals of history. He is now Gaelor the Housebreaker."

Jace let out a dark chuckle. "It hardly matters who gave the order in this room. The entire realm will believe Gaelor Targaryen gave it. For centuries, bards will sing of the Oldtown Massacre. We will wear the blood of this blunder for hundreds of years. And it is all because of this incompetent Housebreaker."
"Keep that tongue to yourself!" Gaelor shouted, rising from his seat.
Jace merely smirked, watching his brother unravel.

The King slammed his fist down. "We cannot undo it! I will not discuss it further!" He took a long drag of his wine. "I will not sit here and mourn the losses of a minor house like Hightower. I will not mourn the betrayal of a man I called friend for thirty years." He looked fiercely at Gaelor. "This incident does not serve us well, but no one—no one—will demand answers from the Targaryens. That is my final word. You are all dismissed."
Jace didn't move. "Father, are we truly not going to discuss the Tyrell backlash? The consequences for the realm?"
"Discuss it amongst yourselves," the King said wearily, standing up. "Cordin, Baelor, Hand... sort this out. Do not bring these petty matters to me."
Baelor stood, removing his reading glasses with a weary sigh. "I am sure Maester Cordin's... assistance will be invaluable."
The King swept out of the room. Gaelor followed a moment later, shooting a look of pure murder at Jace.

Jace, Baelor, and Cordin remained in the chambers until nightfall. They debated endlessly but reached a hollow consensus: they would ease the taxation on the Tyrells slightly, but they would offer no formal apology. Cordin agreed with every word Baelor spoke.
When it was done, Jace went to his mother’s chambers.

Queen Jaery looked like a ghost. Her eyes were bloodshot, her face pale with terror. "Jace," she wept as he entered, "I don't know what to say anymore. There is no misery like the Red Keep. I am going to die in this place... I am going to die because of your father."
"I know, Mother," Jace said softly, sitting beside her.
"Gaelor could not have given that order," she sobbed, clinging to his sleeve.

"I am going to purge every sycophant around my father, one by one," Jace promised, his voice like ice. "At best, Gaelor will never see the throne. If I have my way, he will never set foot in the Red Keep again."
The Queen cried harder. "Why? Why have you all turned on each other? Where is Visenor? You cannot even tell your father that his son fled because he hates him."
"My brother did not flee because he didn't love you," Jace said gently.
"Who could possibly love your father?" the Queen choked out.
Jace’s expression faltered. He looked down at the floor. "He loves you. At least know that."
"Damn his love!" Jaery cried. She gripped Jace's hands. "Have you heard anything of Visenor?"
The Queen looked up at him, her eyes desperate. "May I ask something of you? Please... talk to your brother. Talk to Gaelor. For me."
Jace’s jaw tightened. "I have nothing to say to him."
"Please, Jace. I just need to see my sons speaking to one another. Please." She broke down into racking sobs.
Jace gently stroked his mother's silver hair. "Do not worry. I am here."
He left her chambers and sought out the heir. Just as he found Gaelor in the corridor, the King turned the corner at the far end of the hall.
Gaelor sneered as Jace approached. "Did you come here to get yourself killed? I am already angry enough."

"Of course you are," Jace retorted smoothly. "Executing a massacre that will be spoken of for centuries must be terribly exhausting. But I see you're eating and drinking well enough." Jace lowered his voice. "I am only here for Mother's sake. Otherwise, I would have nothing to say to you."

Gaelor’s face flushed. "It was an accident! I am not pleased with it! My father's methods are brutal, I know, but I followed an order and a tragedy occurred. Do you enjoy standing there acting as if I butchered that girl for sport? Does it soothe your conscience?"

"The blood of those people will stain your hands forever," Jace said coldly. "I didn't expect you to feel remorse, so you haven't surprised me. But if you do not learn to know your place... I will end you."
"What's your problem, you annoying little bitch?" Gaelor spat.
Jace just smirked over his shoulder and began to walk away.
Gaelor lunged, grabbing Jace fiercely by the arm. "As much of a pest as you've been, I have always loved you, Jace! What is your problem with me? There has to be something deeper!"
Jace slowly turned his head, his violet eyes lethal. "If you ever touch me again, it will be your end. Mother is watching us from the balcony. Smile, and walk away."
The King entered the Queen’s chambers holding a flagon of wine and two goblets. He sat heavily across from her.
"Why do you not come to my bed?" the King asked, his speech slightly slurred. "Where have you been these past three days?"
"I am in mourning," Jaery replied, her voice trembling.
"Why?" the King scoffed. "Did your children die?"
Jaery burst into tears. "How can you speak like this? What have you become? Were you always this monster?"
"Gaelor made a mistake!" the King roared defensively. "Is it the first time blood has been spilled in the Seven Kingdoms? I did not give the order to kill the girl!"
"You ordered the death of a fifteen-year-old boy!"
"Such orders were given for me when I was young! What is fifteen years?"
"Is that supposed to comfort me?" Jaery cried.
The King slammed his goblet on the table. "You will return to my chambers tonight. It is your duty to stand by my side. I am not just your King, I am your husband!"

"I do not want to be with my husband anymore," Jaery said, her voice dropping to a harsh whisper. "You are not the man I knew. You were never the greatest king, or the greatest husband, or the greatest father..."
"I do as I was taught!" the King bellowed.
The King stood up, his massive frame looming over her. "What you speak is treason."
"Your sons are fleeing from you, and now you decide you want my love?"
Jaery let out a bitter, broken laugh. "Do you truly expect me to believe that?"
"Watch your words, woman, or I will have your head!" the King roared.
"Because I know you love me!" the King yelled. "Because the difference in our marriage is that you chose me, but I was always forced into this!"
Jaery stared at him, her heart breaking. "Your sons run from you. Visenor took his wife and fled to escape you."
The King hurled his silver goblet against the stone wall. It shattered with a deafening crash. "He did not flee because of me!"
"He fled because no one wants to stand beside you!" Jaery screamed back. "Look at your remaining sons! They are tearing each other apart out there! The next step is them fleeing from you too!"
Blinded by rage, the King struck her. The backhand sent Queen Jaery crashing to the floor.

Breathing heavily, the King stormed out of the room. He marched blindly through the corridors until he saw Jace and Gaelor standing together. He ripped the Valyrian steel blade, Blackfyre, from his hip and charged at them.

"Do something!" the King screamed, leveling the dark, rippling steel at his two sons. "You two maniacs! Did you decide to plague me too? What will you do? Will you run away like your brother? Is that what you are plotting?"
He whipped the blade toward Gaelor. "I gave you Blackfyre! Was this your plan all along? Did I raise you to be such cowards?"
Jace didn't move a muscle. "What is the meaning of this?"
"What are you talking about?" Gaelor stammered, raising his hands.
The King looked them up and down, swaying slightly. "I know I cannot trust you. You are my sons, you should be grateful, yet all you do is cause me problems!"
He turned the legendary sword on Jace, pressing the razor-sharp Valyrian steel against his Hand's cheek. A thin line of crimson blood welled up, sliding down Jace's jaw.

Jace stared blankly into his father's mad eyes, utterly devoid of fear. "Father, what have I ever done but oppose your senseless problems? You are looking for a problem? The problem is standing right there." Jace pointed a finger at Gaelor.
"What is wrong with you two?!" the King roared at the heavens.
"We were just discussing that," Gaelor muttered.
Jace kept his eyes locked on his father. "Why do you care now?"
"When have I never cared for you?!" the King demanded, his breath reeking of arbor gold.
"When have you ever cared?" Jace shot back.
"I made you Hand of the King!" the King bellowed. "I elevated you to the highest station in the realm! Do you covet my crown too? What more do you want from me?"
Queen Jaery rushed into the corridor, weeping in terror. "Please! Stop! The guards will come!"

Jace ignored her, his voice deadly calm. "I am lucky, yes. But even if I were just any Targaryen, I would have eaten from gold plates and slept in silk. In my seventeen years of life, the only thing you ever gave me was this pin. And you gave it to me for a duty. Not out of love. You look at us as tasks to be managed."

Jace didn't flinch as the blade remained pressed to his skin. "You looked at Visenor as a duty, and he fled. You gave a duty to this incompetent fool," he nodded toward Gaelor, "and he committed a massacre. You have only ever viewed us as duties."
The King stood frozen, trembling with rage and drunken sorrow.
A small voice broke the heavy silence. "Let's all go for a walk."
Little Vhaemys stood in the hallway, looking up at the drawn sword.
The King slowly lowered Blackfyre, handing it blindly to Gaelor. He stumbled backward, his voice cracking. "Visenor didn't leave because of me."
He turned and walked away into the shadows of the keep.

The Queen collapsed against the wall, sobbing into her hands. Vhaemys ran to her, hugging her legs. She looked back at Jace and Gaelor. "Why do you treat Father this way?" she asked accusingly. She pointed at Gaelor. "You're going to be King one day. Why do you do this?"
Jace and Gaelor both stepped forward, awkwardly trying to soothe the girl. Jace eventually took Vhaemys by the hand and led her away down the corridor.
Gaelor turned to his weeping mother. "What happened? Is everything alright?"

"I just wanted to leave you two alone for five minutes so you could speak," she sobbed. "My foolish head... you can't accomplish a single thing. I lost one son, am I to lose you two as well? Are the politics of the realm more important than your blood?"

Gaelor’s face hardened. "I don't know what Jace's problem is, but he attacks me at every turn. If terrible things happen to this family in the future... I will not be the cause. I will only fight for our family's happiness."

Baelor turned the corner and saw the Queen weeping on the stone floor. He rushed forward, gripping her gently by the shoulders. "My Queen, I beg of you... return to the King. Do not leave him alone tonight. Spouses must stand by one another."
Defeated, Queen Jaery wiped her tears and returned to the King’s chambers.
Further down the hall, Jace walked in silence with Vhaemys. The little girl was sullen. "You always do this," Vhaemys mumbled.
Jace sighed. "It is not just us, Vhaemys. Father is a difficult man."

Vhaemys suddenly stopped. Her eyes locked onto a large beetle crawling across the ancient stone floor. Without a word, the girl raised her boot and stomped down hard, crushing the insect with a sickening crunch.
Jace stopped. "Why did you crush it?"
Vhaemys looked up, her face innocent and blank. "Do you like bugs now? The maids kill them when they see them."

Jace knelt down to be at eye level with his little brother. "We are the family chosen by the gods, Vhaemys. We are responsible for all living things in the realm. Of course, if they enter our chambers and bother us, we crush their heads. But when they are harmless, wandering in their own space, we do not hurt them."
Vhaemys stared at the crushed shell on the floor. "But this is my space."
Jace looked into the girl's cold, dark eyes. "It is theirs, too."

A week of suffocating silence descended upon the royal family, broken only by the scratching of quills and the distant, rhythmic tolling of the city bells. As the seventh moon waned into the seventh, the Red Keep fell into a grim, bureaucratic routine, the initial fire of the massacre settling into a cold, persistent ash.

No one in the royal family spoke to one another. The only conversations of note were between Jace and Baelor. They dispatched a raven to Highgarden: The toll has been paid in full. We are slightly easing the sanctions. It was a decree utterly devoid of apology.
The children had not seen the King in nearly a week. He remained in his chambers, sleeping beside Jaery, though the two never exchanged a word.

When the smallfolk came to air their grievances, it was the Hand who spoke for the Crown. The King merely sat on the Iron Throne, listening in a drunken stupor. It was the only time Jace and his father shared the same room.

During a brief council meeting, Baelor addressed the crown's overflowing coffers. "We have collected heavy taxes. If we are to sail for the Stepstones, we have more than enough. I suggest we distribute a portion back to the realm to ease the unrest."
He waited for the King to dictate the sum.
The King waved a dismissive hand, his eyes glazed with wine. "Mhm. Fine. You handle it." He looked at the Master of Coin. "Think on it with Tion. Resolve it."
The King turned a lazy gaze to Jace. "You can handle this, can you not?"
"Of course, Your Grace, we will handle it," Baelor interjected smoothly.
Maester Cordin leaned forward. "Will you not specify the amount, Your Grace?"
The King stood up, unsteady on his feet. "You are three capable men. You will figure it out. If there is nothing else, I am leaving." He turned and walked out of the hall.

On the sixth day, in the distant sanctuary of Starfall, Visenor and Rhaella spent their hours reading, drinking, eating, and making love in the quiet warmth of Dorne. Visenor’s smile was still missing a few teeth, but the peace of the mountains suited him.
While sorting through the chaotic archives, Visenor uncovered another of Baelor’s letters. It was half-torn and unmailed.
Visenor’s eyes widened as he read the fading ink:

"...I can offer you my own research, though much of it remains incomplete. A great portion of it I left in Oldtown, and I do not intend to retrieve it. I believe no man has studied our own House more thoroughly than I. I keep certain journals and findings in Oldtown that I value too highly to risk sending by raven—texts I acquired from Dorne and beyond. I am currently studying the nature of the Dreamers. Its absolute truth remains elusive, but I believe I possess documents originating from Old Valyria itself."
Visenor leaned closer to the parchment, utterly captivated.

"I have spent a great deal of time studying Daenys Targaryen," the letter continued. "In the grand tapestry of our history, I believe she is far more deserving of study than even Aegon the Conqueror. Scholars of Old Valyria are rarely taken seriously; men think they deal only in myth and prophecy. But I possess genuine documents, rigorous studies, and dangerous ideas. I do not shy away from speaking of them, for most men either disbelieve them or ignore them because they do not concern Aegon's conquest. We are not the only family to survive the Doom of Old Valyria..."
Visenor traced the words with his finger, his mind racing with the implications.

The rhythmic clatter of training swords echoed through the Red Keep’s yard. Jace wiped sweat from his brow, breathing heavily. After weeks of relentless training, he had transformed into a genuinely capable duelist. He couldn't disarm Ser Orwell Morrigen, but he could hold his own, meeting the knight's strikes with solid parries and rapid footwork.
"You have improved remarkably, my Prince," Morrigen praised, lowering his practice blade. "You could earn your knighthood soon."
"It is an honor to hear that from you, Ser," Jace replied, leaning on his sword.
Morrigen offered a smug, approving smile.
"Do you think I could beat Ser Alester?" Jace asked, half-joking.
Morrigen laughed out loud. "Not quite that much, my Prince."

"I trained him, didn't I? I could beat him." Morrigen chuckled, then grew serious. "Truthfully, in my youth, perhaps. But men my age can no longer stand toe-to-toe with the young blood. They are too fast, too restless. But in the end... experience is everything. Experience triumphs over youth and speed. The men you truly must fear are the experienced ones."
Suddenly, the heavy iron bells of the Red Keep began to toll. The great gates groaned open.
Lady Dayne’s diplomatic envoy was arriving.
Jace handed his training sword to a squire and sprinted across the courtyard. He saw the Dornish banners, and then, riding near the rear of the column, a knight in a battered white cloak.
Ser Alester saw the Hand running toward him and quickly dismounted.
Jace collided with his sworn shield, wrapping his arms around Alester in a fierce, desperate hug. Alester hissed through his teeth, the pain in his bruised ribs flaring, but he returned the embrace.
Realizing the eyes of the court were upon them, Jace quickly stepped back, hiding the profound relief behind a mask of aristocratic indifference.

Alester understood. Despite his shattered face and broken body, the knight slowly lowered himself to one knee. He drew his longsword, planting the tip into the dirt of the courtyard, and bowed his head in perfect, silent loyalty to his Prince.

title: Chapter VII: The Climb and the Kneel
synopsis: As Visenor braves the Dornish peaks for a dangerous birthright, unexpected bonds form within the Red Keep. While the Crown Prince finds rare hope, Jacaelon embraces the solemn vows of a knight.
content: The black walnut stain had settled well.

Visenor studied his reflection in the polished steel one last time before fastening his sword belt. Where silver-gold Valyrian hair had once betrayed him at a glance, a short, dark cut now framed his face. Beside him, Rhaella's own hair had taken on the deep copper-red hue of henna. They looked less like dragonlords and more like travelers from the marches, though their violet eyes remained impossible to disguise.
"The Red Mountains do not forgive fools," Rhaella said quietly.
He smiled, though there was little joy behind it. "That has always been true."
She crossed the room and adjusted the clasp of his cloak herself. "Then tell me why this cannot wait."
Visenor's gaze drifted toward the window where the pale towers of Starfall overlooked the Torrentine.
"Because one day," he answered, "I will return to King's Landing."

"And when that day comes, no one will spare a second glance for a dragonless Targaryen. A prince without a dragon is merely another claimant with silver blood." His fingers closed around the pommel of his sword. "If I have a dragon... my claim carries weight."
She searched his face for several long moments before sighing.
Then Rhaella stepped forward, kissed him softly, and rested her forehead against his.
Outside, the stable boys had already saddled his horse.

Roach stamped impatiently upon the stone yard, a sturdy brown gelding with a broad white rump and more endurance than beauty. Visenor secured his packs himself—food enough for several days, fresh waterskins, rope, climbing gear, blankets, oilcloth, torches, spare flint, and every other necessity he could think to carry.
Rhaella stood upon the steps until he disappeared beyond the gates of Starfall.
The Red Mountains rose from Dorne like the jagged spine of some forgotten beast.
By noon the land had become harsher. By afternoon, harsher still.

Roach carried him as far as any horse reasonably could, climbing narrow trails where loose stones rattled beneath iron shoes and every gust of mountain wind smelled of dust and sunbaked rock. Eagles circled high above. Wild goats watched from impossible ledges before vanishing among the cliffs.
When the sun finally neared the western horizon, Visenor found a sheltered hollow beneath an outcrop and tied Roach where sparse grass still clung stubbornly to life.
The climb was worse than he had imagined.

Loose shale slipped beneath every step. Sharp stone tore at his gloves. More than once he found himself crawling instead of walking, fingers searching desperately for handholds where none seemed to exist.
By the middle of the second day, every muscle in his body protested.

He sat upon a broad shelf of stone overlooking the valleys below and uncorked a flask of sweet Dornish wine infused with fragrant spices. It warmed his throat as he watched the evening sun paint the mountains crimson.
For the first time since leaving Starfall, his thoughts drifted elsewhere.
To the dragons he had never possessed.
To everything he had lost before he had ever been old enough to understand it.
A sharp scrape upon stone pulled him from his thoughts.
Not thirty paces away stood a mountain goat.
It watched him with pale, unblinking eyes.
Then the goat backed away several steps.
He picked up a loose pebble and tossed it lazily toward the animal.
The stone skipped harmlessly across the ground.
Only to wheel around a heartbeat later and charge.
Steel hissed free of its scabbard.
The beast came downhill like an avalanche.
At the last instant Visenor stepped aside.
Rather than striking flesh, the edge glanced off one thick horn with a loud crack.
The impact threw the goat sideways across the slope.
This time his swing met the horn even harder.
Again the animal was thrown aside.
Finally he raised his sword toward it.
His shout echoed across the cliffs.
Whether from the sound or simple frustration, the goat turned away at last, climbing higher into the mountains with slow, deliberate steps.
Every few moments it glanced back.
Only when it disappeared beyond a ridge did Visenor sheath his sword.
He did not enjoy the feeling that he was now the one being followed.
Its entrance was narrow, shadowed, and silent.

Visenor examined it from a distance before deciding against entering in darkness. Instead he built a small camp just outside, keeping the fire low enough that its light would not travel far across the mountains.
By the fifth hour of morning he finally lit a torch.
Sword in hand, he stepped inside.
The cave smelled of damp stone.
A few dozen paces inward he found the remains of a man.
Only bones remained, picked clean long ago. Beside the skeleton rested a small hand axe whose wooden haft had long since rotted away.
There was no one left to hear the words.
He recovered the axe head before continuing deeper.
A sudden shadow crossed the cave entrance.
Its silhouette lingered for several seconds before disappearing again.
Visenor waited nearly half an hour before moving once more.
Eventually he emerged from the cave and continued climbing.
Ten minutes of climbing left his lungs burning.
Just ahead lay the blackened corpse of a mountain goat.
The hide had been scorched nearly to ash.
Not far beyond the carcass stood another cave.
Massive fallen stones framed its entrance.
Visenor remained hidden behind a ridge for several minutes, studying every shadow.
At last he lit another torch.
The cavern stretched farther than he expected.
Far within the darkness rested a dragon egg.
His eyes swept across the chamber.
A low rumble rolled through the cave.
He backed away one careful step.
Dust cascaded from ancient scales as the creature rose from where it had concealed itself against the cavern wall.
Its scales shimmered with pale yellow touched by old bone.
Visenor did not reach for his sword.
The blade clattered softly onto the cave floor.
One heartbeat it stood across the cavern.
The next it was only a few feet away.
Every instinct screamed at him to run.
He forced himself to remain still.
"I will not harm you," he whispered in High Valyrian. "Rytsas."
Then the scales along its throat began to glow.
The entire cavern flickered beneath the growing light.
The name escaped him almost without thought.
With a sudden shove of its snout, it struck Visenor square in the chest.
He was thrown backward, tumbling across the stone until he landed outside the cave entrance.
The dragon followed only as far as the mouth of the cavern.
Visenor slowly rose, keeping his empty hands visible.
"I did not come to command you," he called in High Valyrian. "I only wish to camp nearby. I mean you no harm. I even left my sword behind."
The dragon regarded him in silence.
And vanished once more into the darkness.
Visenor stood there for a long while before allowing himself to breathe again.
That evening he built his camp within sight of the cave.
He did not attempt to enter again.
The journey had taken him deep into the jagged heart of the Red Mountains, a three-day ride followed by two days of agonizing, breathless climbing.
And now, he simply had to wait.
When the mountain winds grew quiet, he sang old Valyrian songs into the night—not loudly, nor as commands, but as one traveler might sing to another beside a lonely fire.
The bells of King's Landing rang just after sunrise, though they carried little joy these days.

Smoke still drifted above countless chimneys, and the streets below the Red Keep stirred with merchants, gold cloaks, beggars, and hungry mouths. Overhead, dragons wheeled through the pale morning sky, fed well by the Crown and growing larger with every passing moon. One in particular flew lower than the others, banking sharply before unleashing a jet of flame at some unfortunate flock of gulls that had wandered too close to the city walls.

His own dragon had always possessed a savage temper. It burned beetles, birds, and anything else that dared to move before it, yet around its rider it behaved with remarkable obedience, as though violence was simply another form of affection.
"A spirited beast," Saathos observed beside him.
"I'll choose to take that as praise."
Saathos returned no smile. He stepped closer instead, lowering his voice.
"A raven arrived before dawn. I heard the news before it reached the king."
"They found six bodies in the forest. Ser Curtass among them."
"Six men..." he repeated. "Were they killed by men, or did they stumble across some great beast? Curtass was no easy knight to bring down."
"Sword wounds," Saathos replied. "Every one of them."
"The reports say his face was ruined beyond recognition. Whatever happened to the others... something else happened to him."
Gaelor looked toward the city below, thoughtful now.
"The men searching the area found no village, no camp, nothing worth noting. The only place within an hour or so was a sept."
"The septons knew nothing—or claimed to know nothing. No one spoke." Saathos glanced toward the courtyard. "There was, however, a traveler lodging there that night."
"Very little. He recalled hearing horses around the same time Ser Curtass is believed to have died. He also remembered a knight stopping briefly at the sept."
"Whether that knight matters or not remains to be seen."
Before either man could say more, the bells sounded again.
Both men turned toward the castle gates.
A column of riders entered the outer yard beneath fluttering banners of House Dayne.
Gaelor watched the convoy climb toward the keep.
Then he descended the steps to greet them.
Lady Lorenah Dayne pulled back her hood before the horses had fully stopped.
The journey south had left dust upon her cloak and weariness beneath her violet eyes, but neither diminished the effortless grace with which she dismounted.
"It is good to see you again, my lady."
"It was an exhausting road, Prince Jace."
Her voice carried the soft music of Dorne.
"I trust you managed to keep my knight safe."
A faint grin tugged at Lorenah's lips.
"Should you not be asking whether he managed to keep us safe?"
Behind her, Ser Alester allowed himself the smallest of smiles.
Lorenah looked around the castle yard.
"It has been many years since I last came to King's Landing."
More than a few curious eyes had settled upon her.
"You give the city too much credit."
She glanced toward the crowded streets below Aegon's High Hill.
"The roads were full of hungry people. We distributed what food we could spare before reaching the castle."
"And my brothers have done precious little to improve them."
"If you'll forgive me, I'd like a proper bath before supper."
Jace remained where he stood until she disappeared inside.
Only then did he turn toward the knight climbing stiffly from his horse.
Alester looked considerably worse than when he had left.
Several fresh scars disappeared beneath his collar.
His movements betrayed pain despite every effort to hide it.
For a long moment neither man spoke.
Then Jace crossed the distance between them and embraced him.
"I haven't slept properly since I sent you away."
"My prince... did you truly doubt me?"
Jace pulled back enough to meet his eyes.
"I did not trust the men I knew would be following you."
"I'll try not to make a habit of it."
Together they entered the keep, surrendering their horses to the stablemaster before disappearing into the halls of the Red Keep.
By the time Alester had exchanged his travel-worn armor for clean clothes, the servants had withdrawn, leaving the chamber blessedly quiet.
Jace poured wine for them both.
"Well?" Alester asked as he eased himself into a chair. "How fares the capital?"
Jace let out a weary breath.
Alester accepted the cup with a grateful nod.
"We heard rumors on the road. About Prince Gaelor."
Alester merely smiled into his cup.
"I suppose I'd best keep my thoughts to myself."
The knight hesitated only a heartbeat.
"I imagine whatever hopes remained with House Tyrell have died now. I doubt we'll be sharing a hall with them again."
As Alester lifted his cup, something caught his eye.
Pinned upon Jace's doublet was the brooch of the Hand.
Jace followed his gaze before giving a humorless laugh.
"I suspect it's little more than a title." Jace swirled the wine in his cup. "No one seems particularly interested in listening to the Hand."
"If you've figured out my father, I'd dearly love to hear the answer."
For a time they drank in comfortable silence.
"All this time... he was right in front of our nose."
"He was. Right until the moment he breathed his last at my feet."
Alester stared at him for a moment before quietly refilling both cups.
Jace waved a hand dismissively after telling him about how they uncovered the plot constructed by the Tyrells and the late Hand.
"Enough about King's Landing. Tell me everything."
Concern crossed Alester's face, though he quickly hid it.
After a pause, he said quietly,
"I did what had to be done."
"I had already sent Prince Visenor ahead. The princess was still with us. We couldn't risk both of them."
"For Her Grace's safety." Alester glanced toward the scars still healing beneath his sleeves and gave a small, self-deprecating smile. "In hindsight... perhaps not my finest decision."
Jace shook his head in disbelief.
"Gods... did you truly fight them all alone?"
"I've never fought a battle like that before."
His voice remained calm, but Jace noticed the distant look settling into his eyes.
"It was the hardest fight of my life."
"The fact you're sitting here at all is a miracle."
"We hadn't been inside Starfall half an hour before we were found."
"I entrusted Prince Visenor to her care."
"I made certain she understood you sent me there to put Prince Visenor under her protection."
Jace considered that for several moments.
"We didn't have the luxury of certainty."
"I only regret we failed to remain hidden."
"You completed a journey few men in Westeros could have."
"I fear I also embarrassed myself rather spectacularly along the way."
"But besides that?" Jace reached over and lightly tapped one of Alester's bruised shoulders. "I'd say you did rather well."
Seeing it eased something inside Jace he had not realized he had been carrying.
"How are Prince Visenor and Princess Rhaella?" he asked.
Jace released a breath he hadn't known he was holding.
A thoughtful silence followed before Jace spoke again.
"The less correspondence between Starfall and King's Landing, the better."
Jace looked toward the window overlooking the city.
"But sooner or later we'll have no choice."
"I imagine that discussion now belongs to you... and Lady Lorenah."
For a moment neither man spoke.
"May the gods look kindly upon us."
After another drink, Jace set his cup aside and stood.
Jace left him alone to sleep, though as the door closed behind him, he found himself wondering whether either of them truly believed the hardest days were behind them.
The sun had begun its slow descent when Jace found Lady Lorenah strolling through the gardens overlooking Blackwater Bay.

She had exchanged the dust of the road for a gown of pale lavender, though there remained something unmistakably Dornish in the ease with which she carried herself. The gardens of the Red Keep were carefully tended, every hedge trimmed and every flower placed with purpose. Beautiful, certainly—but ordered.
"You seem well rested," Jace said as he approached.
She turned toward him with a small smile.
"I'll happily take that as a compliment."
"The castle is." Her eyes wandered toward the towering walls surrounding them. "The city beyond them... less so."
"They say it's unlike anywhere else in Westeros."
He looked at her as though she had suggested a stroll beyond the Wall.
"If we're going, you'll stop calling me that."
"We're hardly going to blend in if you keep addressing me as my lady every other sentence."
Jace rubbed the back of his neck.
"You've also always wanted to survive it, I hope."
"They also say many people are robbed there before sunset."
"My brother has given the smallfolk every reason to resent this family."
Then Lorenah reached into the folds of her cloak.
She unfolded a length of finely woven black cloth embroidered with silver Dornish patterns.
She raised an eyebrow, glancing knowingly toward his newly darkened hair.
"If we truly mean to disappear into Flea Bottom..."
"...then perhaps neither of us should be dressed like nobility."
"I was about to suggest the same."
"I never imagined I'd hear a Dayne ask for rags."
"So," she said, turning toward the castle.
Jace folded the shawl over one arm.
"I know a place that serves tolerable fish."
"In Flea Bottom that's practically a royal feast."
Together they walked back toward the keep to prepare, leaving behind the carefully trimmed gardens for a city that smelled of smoke, salt, mud, and life.
Far above them, dragons circled lazily over King's Landing, their shadows drifting across the towers like passing clouds.
That same afternoon, another corner of the Red Keep was filled not with politics or whispered reports, but with tears.
Naella hurried through the corridors with scarcely a thought for courtly decorum. The moment she caught sight of Gaelor, she broke into a run.
Before he could ask what had happened, she had thrown her arms around him.
She pulled back just enough for him to see the tears glistening in her eyes—not tears of grief.
"My prince..." she whispered, smiling through them. "I'm with child."
For a heartbeat, it was as though he had forgotten how to speak.
"Your seed is strong... just like you."
A broad grin spread across his face before he kissed her.
The noise of the castle seemed to vanish around them.
When they finally parted, Gaelor rested both hands upon her shoulders.
"This..." He laughed in disbelief. "This is wonderful."
"In times like these..." His smile only widened. "This may be the best news Father's heard in months."
Still hand in hand, they made for the king's solar.
The guards outside moved to stop them.
The guards exchanged uncertain looks before reluctantly allowing them through.
Inside, King Baelenys was already listening to a messenger.
The king lifted one hand as Gaelor entered.
"...our search led us to a nearby sept, Your Grace. The men stationed there knew little, though one traveler claimed a knight had stopped there shortly before your man was attacked."
"Ser Curtass Whent is dead, Your Grace."
"It appears he died carrying out the task you assigned him."
"He died doing his duty. See that the proper honors are given to him and be done with it."
"Your Grace... perhaps there is still value in learning who—"
Then Baelor, who had remained quiet until now, finally spoke.
"Send someone to that sept. If Whent died for something, we might as well discover what."
Baelor considered for only a moment.
He looked back to the messenger.
The man bowed deeply before withdrawing.
Only then did Gaelor's excitement return.
For the first time since entering the room, surprise touched the king's face.
Then, with unexpected gentleness, he reached out and patted her cheek.
He gave Gaelor a single approving nod.
"See that your princess is well cared for."
And just as quickly as the moment had come...
...the king walked past them and left the room.
Gaelor watched him go before looking back to Naella.
She slipped her hand into his once more.
"And stop letting your brother trouble your heart."
Gaelor found he had no answer.
The disguise suited neither of them.

She had wrapped a faded shawl around her head until only her violet eyes remained visible. Jace wore a coarse hood over his silver hair, his clothes exchanged for worn wool and patched linen borrowed from castle servants. No prince had ever looked quite so uncomfortable trying to resemble a common laborer.
"You still walk like someone who's never carried a bucket in his life," Lorenah observed.

By the time they descended into Flea Bottom, the evening streets had begun to swell with life. Musicians played battered fiddles outside alehouses while children darted through muddy alleys with impossible speed. Hawkers shouted over one another, selling everything from roasted onions to chipped daggers, old boots, and charms promising fortune or fertility.
The smell was another matter entirely.
Smoke, fish, stale ale, sweat, damp timber, and humanity blended into something uniquely King's Landing.
"You may be the only noblewoman in Westeros to say that."
A fire breather had gathered a small crowd near an alley mouth. He swallowed another mouthful of oil before sending a great plume of flame roaring into the twilight.
Lorenah leaned forward with delighted laughter.
"Now that would never be allowed at court."
A little farther along, an elderly woman sat behind a rough wooden table covered in polished stones of every imaginable color.
"Magic gems!" she cried. "Lucky gems! Gems blessed by the Seven!"
"They will be the moment you wear one."
She looked back at him with mock disapproval.
"And my lady will not be buying anything."
"What would you have me call you?"
She looked him up and down thoughtfully.
She stared at him for a heartbeat before bursting into laughter.
"I wasn't aware I'd ever possessed them."
"I believe King's Landing has finally driven you mad."
They continued deeper into the district until Jace stopped outside a narrow alehouse.
"In Flea Bottom that's practically a royal feast."
Inside, the benches were crowded and the air thick with laughter and spilled ale.
They were served a pair of rough wooden bowls filled with fish stew, dark bread, and cheap ale.
A moment later they were both eating like everyone else.
Some broth found its way onto Lorenah's cheek.
Without thinking, Jace reached across the table and brushed it away with his thumb.
Then a small body collided gently with Lorenah's shoulder.
A boy—perhaps eight years old—froze where he stood.
He had never seen eyes that color before.
The boy mumbled something too quietly for Jace to catch.
"I'm Dany," she replied with a playful smile before taking the child's hand. "Shall I read your fortune?"
She studied his tiny palm with exaggerated seriousness.
She traced one line with a finger.
He fumbled at his pocket, clearly searching for a coin.
Finding none, he looked down in embarrassment.
The boy beamed before disappearing back into the crowd.
"I ought to come here more often."
"There is nothing like this in Starfall."
"Dorne has places with life..." she said, looking around the crowded room. "But Starfall is peaceful."
They wandered long after the meal was finished.

The crowds thinned as the hour grew later, leaving behind quieter lanes where the noise of taverns became a distant murmur and lantern light spilled across uneven cobblestones. Here and there, voices drifted from open windows, mingling with the strains of some old song carried on the evening breeze.
Lorenah walked a step ahead, her shawl still concealing all but her eyes.
"So," she said at last, slowing her pace. "Tell me something."
"Why did you send your brother to me?"
"And why," she continued, "did you trust me in particular?"
He was silent for a moment.
"You made quite an impression the day we met."
"No one speaks to a Targaryen prince the way you do."
"Which is precisely why it stood out."
"Or perhaps Ser Alester Dayne is simply a very clever man."
"The moment he arrived in Dorne, he became... nervous."
"He avoided my eyes. Chose his words too carefully." She looked toward him knowingly. "It made me wonder."
"Did Prince Jace truly intend to entrust his brother to me..."
"...or did Ser Alester simply have the misfortune of being discovered the moment he crossed into Dorne?"
After a brief silence she continued.
"No word of your brother will leave Starfall."
Lorenah looked up at the stars beginning to appear overhead.
"Our kingdoms have shed enough blood to drown rivers."
"By every measure..." she said quietly, "I ought to be your enemy."
That drew another laugh from her.
"Perhaps," she said after a moment, "we could be the ones to end that."
Jace looked toward the lights stretching across King's Landing.
"Peace wouldn't be the worst thing to happen to this realm."
She stepped closer until they stood side by side beneath the open sky.
"Why must we always find reasons to hate one another?"
"I wish my father asked himself the same question."
"The only language he truly understands is conquest."
Lorenah regarded him for a long moment before humming a quiet Dornish melody as they resumed walking.
"How long do you intend for me to remain in King's Landing?"
"I had hoped..." Jace said carefully, "...that once things settled somewhat, we might hold a tourney."
"It would do the city some good."
"Do you think Prince Gaelor could resist?"
"I've heard enough about him to doubt it."
She looked at him over her shoulder.
"I shall, if I could have your favor."
By the time they reached the castle gates, the city had fallen quiet beneath the night.
Jace escorted her to the chambers prepared for House Dayne.
"I've never had an evening quite like this."
"You've never wandered Flea Bottom in disguise?"
"Then perhaps," she said softly, "we've both gained something tonight."
Before he could answer, she rose onto her toes and placed a light kiss at the corner of his mouth.
Then she slipped inside, leaving him standing speechless in the corridor.
For several heartbeats, he simply stared at the closed door.
Only then did the smile spread across his face.
He had scarcely gone a dozen paces before another familiar voice interrupted his thoughts.
Princess Saera stood beneath the torchlight, studying him with quiet amusement.
"And if Ser Alester wishes to see the king's daughter," she replied with the faintest smile, "he knows perfectly well where to find her."
With that, she continued down the corridor, leaving Jace chuckling to himself.
There was one knight who deserved waking.
A weary voice came from within.
Alester was seated on the edge of the bed, already dressed once more, though his injuries still protested every movement.
That was enough to draw the knight's attention.
"I imagine she'd like to see you."
Alester gave a small, uncertain smile.
He glanced at the fading bruises across his face in the polished steel mirror.
"I wasn't certain presenting myself like... this... would be appropriate."
