Jace leaned casually against the doorframe and continued.
Silence settled for a moment before Alester asked,
"I need you to teach me jousting."
"The Lord Commander believes I'm ready. But what about you?"
"Tomorrow morning we could go together and Ser Morrigen can knight you."
Jace stepped into the room and quietly shut the door behind him.
"I cannot simply knight a prince in the middle of his bedchamber."
"I have no need for a gallery of lords or the gaze of the court. To kneel before Ser Alester Dayne is an honor; to be raised by my brother’s hand is the only blessing I require."
Jace had already dropped to one knee.
Realization struck Alester all at once.
He immediately climbed back to his feet.
The familiar steel caught the candlelight as he rested its flat gently upon Jace's shoulder.
His expression, moments ago embarrassed, became solemn.
He recited the ancient vows every knight of the Seven Kingdoms knew by heart.
"In the name of the Warrior, I charge you to be brave."
"In the name of the Father, I charge you to be just."
"In the name of the Mother, I charge you to defend the young and innocent."
When the final words had been spoken, he lifted the blade. A faint smile touched his lips.
"Arise, Ser Jacaelon, a knight of the Seven Kingdoms."
For a moment he could say nothing.
Alester noticed before politely pretending not to.
After another quiet moment, Alester sheathed his sword.
"I suppose I should finally visit the princess."
"I believe she'd pretend not to have been waiting."
Jace remained behind only long enough to remove his boots before finally lying down, the words Rise, Ser Jacaelon still echoing in his thoughts.
As Alester crossed the silent corridors, a faint glow beneath one door caught his eye.
The door opened almost at once.
Saera studied him from head to toe without speaking.
"...They did quite a number on you."
"So perhaps my brother exaggerated your invincibility."
"And yet many knights forget their vows."
Her violet eyes searched his face.
For the first time that evening, she smiled.
"...Though perhaps not quite as glad as my brother."
"Before I left, we used to walk together whenever time allowed."
Saera considered him for several moments.
They stepped into the corridor together.
From farther down the gallery, unnoticed behind a stone pillar, Jace watched them disappear into the torchlit halls.

Only after they had vanished from sight did he turn away, wearing the quiet smile of a brother who believed, if only for one evening, that perhaps the gods had shown his family a small measure of mercy.
For two days, the dragon never left his thoughts.

Visenor made no attempt to enter the cave again. Each morning he woke before dawn, tended his fire, and waited. Sometimes he spoke in High Valyrian. Sometimes he sang the old songs of the Freehold, letting the melodies drift through the mountain air without expectation of an answer. Other times he simply sat in silence, content to let the dragon know he had not come as a hunter.
Shortly after sunrise on the seventh day, the silence broke.

The dragon threw back its head and loosed a thunderous roar that rolled through the cliffs. Its yellowed scales caught the morning light like old ivory, and smoke curled lazily from its nostrils as it fixed its gaze upon the lone Targaryen who had refused to leave.
He kept his hands where the dragon could see them.
Boneskin answered with another low growl.
"I've come to make peace with you," Visenor continued in High Valyrian. "Not to command you."
His voice remained steady, though he stood straighter than before. Not submissive.
He reached into one of his packs and carefully placed strips of dried meat upon a flat stone several paces away before retreating.
Then, without so much as tasting it, the dragon spread its wings.
With a single powerful beat, it launched itself from the mountainside.
Visenor watched until it became little more than a speck against the pale Dornish sky.
Nearly three quarters of an hour passed before the familiar silhouette returned.
Boneskin descended in a wide circle before landing heavily outside the cave.
Something hung limp from its jaws.
The carcass landed at Visenor's feet.
He carved away a piece of meat and ate.
Moments later, Boneskin stepped forward, tore into the opposite side of the carcass, and began feeding beside him.
Man and dragon shared the same meal.
The only sounds were tearing flesh and the distant cry of hawks circling above the peaks.
When the meal was finished, Visenor wiped his hands upon his cloak and slowly extended one arm.
A faint rumble escaped the dragon's throat.
When he was finally close enough, his fingertips brushed the rough, warm scales along the dragon's neck.
The scales felt harder than polished stone, yet warm with the heat of the dragon's blood.
A smile found its way onto Visenor's face.
He had not climbed these mountains expecting to return astride a dragon.
That had never been the purpose.
There would be time enough for greater ambitions.
He remained outside the cave until the afternoon, saying little, allowing the dragon to grow accustomed to his presence. When at last he gathered his belongings, Boneskin watched him in silence.
"I'll return," Visenor promised in High Valyrian.
It simply remained at the cave mouth, its great head tilted ever so slightly as the prince descended the mountain.
This time, the words sounded less like a promise...
...and more like the beginning of one.
Roach greeted him with an indignant snort after nearly four days alone.

The old gelding carried him down the remaining trails far more quickly than they had climbed them. By the time the first light of dawn spilled across the Torrentine, the towers of Starfall stood once more against the brightening sky.
Visenor scarcely waited for the stable hands.
He all but leapt from the saddle and hurried through the castle.
Rhaella found him before he found her.
One look at his face was enough.

Over cups of Dornish wine that soon became a bottle, he recounted every step of the journey—the burned goat, the hidden cave, the dragon egg, the first terrifying meeting with Boneskin, the songs, the waiting, and finally the meal they had shared together.
Long after the wine had run dry, they remained wrapped in one another's arms.
Outside, dawn bathed Starfall in pale gold.
Inside, for the first time in many years, Visenor allowed himself to believe that fate might yet have reserved something extraordinary for him.
